Divine ivory. The greatest single act that can affect a god's glory is the consumption of another god's divine ivory. Divine ivory is the core of a god's essence. For gods with beasts' origins, it is part or all of their skeleton, but for other gods it might be a delicate lattice, a handful of fine particles, or a geometric solid. Regardless of its shape, divine ivory is the strongest material known in Planegea, and if one god consumes the ivory of another, it usually gains glory equal to the consumed god's strata × 100.

When a god consumes divine ivory, its appearance changes, becoming a hybrid its original form and that of the god it devoured. Strangely, this hybrid form is always slightly more humanoid in shape than what came before it. However, this act of divine cannibalism is considered a wicked act by most gods, and the allied gods of a dead deity will often unite their followers to punish any god who transgresses in such a way.

entries.name: Milestone Glory
entries: Divine glory is an abstract system meant to help DMs decide what impact mortal actions might have on the gods, if any. Generally, a particular act will not alter a god's strata, and therefore glory need not be precisely tracked—rather, these values are given to suggest how important an act may or may not be to gods in the area. If you want to prepare an adventure where glory makes a difference to the story, consider either having the god on the verge of changing strata or else use what we like to call Milestone Glory—deciding glory not by individual acts, but rather by major achievements that shift the balance of awe in a particular god's favor.

entries.name: Negotiating with a God
entries: The relationship between a shaman and a god is often transactional, particularly if they are merely negotiating with the god for safe passage. Use the ideas below as inspiration for what a god might ask of a shaman or other supplicant.

entries.caption: What the God Wants
rows: Slay a nearby druid or capture a rogue visitant
Craft a shrine, altar, or memorial in an elevated or magical place
Destroy the shrine of a rival god
Retrieve a magic item the god desires
Resolve a conflict or soothe a dispute with an aggrieved party
Prepare a feast for the god, either symbolic or literal
Slay a beast in their honor or to protect their faithful
Alter the landscape in a way that pleases the god
Slay a rival god and bring back the divine ivory
Make a personal sacrifice of something the character holds dear
Endure a harrowing mystical vigil or vision
Convince local mortals to swear their fealty to the god

entries.name: Creating a God
entries: When running a Planegea campaign, you may want to create your own gods—as a benevolent patron, a deadly adversary, or simply a neutral power in the world. As beings of immense magic and might, gods aren't simple to design, and their ultimate challenge ratings can vary wildly depending on the choices you make. But to enable you as the DM to spin your own adventures into being, this section offers guidance for how to make your very own proto-god stat block.

entries.name: 1. Start with a Name & Concept
entries: To create a god, it's a good idea to start with a name and a big-picture idea of how you want this god to operate in the world, the scope of its power, and the way it interacts with others. Here are some elements to consider:

Strata. How powerful is your god in Planegea? Gods of the 1st strata are gods over only what they can see. 2nd-strata gods rule local areas, like specific forests or hills, and are very common. 3rd-strata gods are rare and rule entire regions, while those of the 4th strata are almost unheard of, and powerful enough to stretch their influence across the world.

Personality. Is your god kind or wrathful, eerie or playful, curious or passive? Is it quick to anger or stoic and unresponsive? Is it quick to grant gifts but also quick to make demands, or does it only reluctantly encounter outsiders? Choosing the overall demeanor of your god will help you make decisions through the rest of this process.

Origin. What was your god at first? In Planegea, gods are not abstract concepts, but creatures, places, or things where the world's magic knots and pools through some quirk of mortal reverence. Where did your god begin and how did it become a god? Was it simply very old and honored by a local clan, or was there a strange and sudden event that brought into godhood?

Hallow. The hallow is the place in the world most shaped by your god's will. Consider its location, layout, and shape. What are its entrances and exits? Is it easy to get to or hard? How do its worshipers get there? Does your god tend to remain in the center of the hallow or prowl its edges? Are there any environmental hazards within the hallow? It may be helpful to sketch a simple map for your notes.

Domain(s). A god's domain is a description of its personality and the types of magic it favors. Some gods are easily described with a single domain, while others have multiple. What domain expresses your god's personality? Do different followers of your god revere different aspects of its nature?

Regional effects. Gods of different strata can influence the world beyond their hallow to different extents. How does your god affect the world within its reach? Does it bless the area surrounding its hallow or curse it? Do its visitants regularly roam the land, or do plants and animals wither and creep far away from its seat of power? How is the water and air affected by the god's nature? Are there particular creatures it favors that thrive near it?

entries.name: Example: Making Gheim Shadow-Whisper
entries: Let's make a god called Gheim Shadow-Whisper. We'll make him a 2nd-strata god, because we want him to rule part of the Cult Riverlands as a villainous idol that our PCs will eventually encounter.
His name sounds ominous, so let's go ahead and say that's his personality: he's creepy and loves to cause fear and dismay in his cult and especially outsiders.

For his origin, let's use the name as our inspiration… maybe he's literally a whispering shadow from the interior of a cave. Imagine a cave, carved by a long-dried river, where dark magic pooled and the shadows started to whisper secrets and threats. Over time, it became so feared that it became a god.

For his hallow, we'll choose an existing map of a small cave system. Let's say the map shows some deep, dark pits, has three entrances, and we'll say it sits in a little canyon. That'll do for a hallow for now.
Let's choose Trickery and Death for his domains. Since we want Gheim Shadow-Whisper to be our villain, those should give us good options for both him and his followers.

Lastly, what regional effects should Gheim give us? Well, his name suggests an obvious one—when you stand in shadows within his reach, you hear whispering voices, whose words you can't quite make out. Let's say also that birds and beasts nearby seem frightened and thin, and when you sleep in his reach, you're prey to bad dreams of whispering dark.

entries.name: 2. Choose a Base Stat Block
entries: Now that you have an overall schema for your god, the next stage is to build a stat block so your characters can encounter the deity. Note that not every god needs a stat block—some gods are so powerful, mysterious, or distant that only a name is required. But if your characters are going to fight a god or enter its hallow, it's a good idea to work out the mechanics of its interaction with them—that's what steps 2 thru 9 are for.

To get started, choose an existing creature with features and abilities that fit the vibe of the god you're creating. Don't worry about appearance, creature type, or alignment yet—we'll get to those. Instead, look at creatures by CR and think about whether their attacks and abilities match up with your concept. If you want a god who's mostly a dominating physical bruiser, you might choose a different stat block than you would for a god full of trickery and mischief.
Look at creatures with these CRs for each strata level:

items: 1st strata: CR 10–15
2nd strata: CR 16–20
3rd strata: CR 21–25
4th strata: CR 26–30

entries: If you are more confident in creature design, you can also use the ability scores of a creature of the recommended CR and put them onto a different stat block. But be aware that has downstream implications, and will require you to recalculate ability checks, saving throw, and attack and damage rolls.

entries.name: Example (continued)
entries: Gheim is 2nd strata, which means we need a CR 16–20 monster. After considering a few options, we're going to go with the Mummy Lord. It's a relatively rare monster to encounter and our players haven't fought one yet, so its abilities should surprise them.
A lot of his spells and abilities will need to be tweaked for our theme, but that's just fine! Most of that can be done with simple flavor changes.

entries.name: 3. Re-skin the Basics
entries: The next step is to adjust the basic elements of the stat block to suit your concept. These changes won't have too much of an impact on the math of your god's stat block, but may require some tinkering.

Size. Most gods appear as larger than life. Consider whether you want to increase the size of your god for visual and story impact. (This will also increase its hit dice: Small, d6; Medium, d8; Large, d10; Huge, d12; Gargantuan, d20.)

Alignment. Choose your god's alignment. Good gods in Planegea have the celestial creature type, and evil gods have the fiend type. Neutral gods are by far the most common, and can have any creature type other than celestial, fiend, humanoid, or giant. Think about law and chaos as they fit your adventure and how they relate to mortal clans—a lawful god will likely exercise more direct dominion and judgment than a chaotic god, who might encourage more individual choice and chance.

Creature type. Now that you have your alignment, change the creature type of your chosen stat block if necessary, and add the (god) tag. Look at other monsters with the same creature type. If they have any standard features such as undead nature, add those (and remove standard features from the creature's former type, if any).

entries.name: Example (continued)
entries: Gheim's Mummy Lord base stat block is Medium undead, lawful evil. We'll make Gheim a Large fiend (god), lawful evil: His lawful nature and his in-world role as a Riverlands idol will make him a devil, not a chaotic demon.

The Mummy Lord doesn't seem to have any features that wouldn't work for Gheim—even his Rejuvenation feature seems like something that could work well, if re-skinned. We'll keep all his features as they are.

entries.name: 4. Add Basic Divine Traits
entries: Gods in Planegea have some basic traits that they all share in common. Add the following, if the base stat block doesn't already have them:
Resistance. Give your god resistance to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing from nonmagical weapons.
Senses. Give your god truesight to a range of 15 feet.

Languages. Give your god telepathy within the hallow, Common, and any languages usually granted by your creature type (such as Celestial for celestials, Abyssal for demons, Infernal for devils, Primordial for elementals, etc).

Charisma score. Gods are powerful forces of personality. Increase your god's Charisma score to a number within a given range, based on their strata: 1st strata, 14–17; 2nd strata, 18–21; 3rd strata, 22–25; 4th strata, 26–30. If their Charisma score is already in this range or higher, no change is necessary.

entries.name: Example (continued)
entries: The Mummy Lord already had plenty of resistances, so we'll leave that alone. We'll add truesight and Infernal, but leave Common off the list. This seems fun because maybe those whispers are in Infernal, which are generally scary and maddening and meaningless to those who don't speak the language. Gheim can use telepathy to communicate with the characters once they enter his hallow. Since the Mummy Lord already had high Charisma, we'll put Gheim's Charisma at 20, near the top of his range.

entries.name: 5. Add Innate Spellcasting
entries: All gods in Planegea have the same spellcasting method—they can innately prepare and cast any spells from shaman (cleric) or guardian (paladin) spell lists, as well as spells granted to clerics of their domain(s). They don't require any material components to cast these spells, and can prepare different spells at the end of a long rest or by using their Gather Magic feature, which we'll look at in the next step.

Use your god's strata to determine the number of spells they can prepare, and choose prepared spells as those it favors in general, special circumstances aside. If your chosen stat block has other innate spellcasting, replace it with these spells.

Your god's spellcasting ability is Charisma. Their spellcasting attack modifier is equal to their Charisma modifier + their proficiency bonus, and their spell save DC is equal to 8 + their Charisma modifier + their proficiency bonus.

items: At-will: Six cantrips, 1st-, or 2nd-level spells
1/Day: Three 3rd-level spells
At-will: Nine cantrips, 1st-, or 2nd-level spells
3/Day: Six 3rd- to 5th-level spells
1/Day: Three 6th-level spells
At-will: Twelve cantrips, 1st-, or 2nd-level spells
6/Day: Nine 3rd- to 6th-level spells
3/Day: Six 7th- to 8th-level spells
1/Day: Three 9th-level spells
At-will: Twelve cantrips or 1st- to 6th-level spells
6/Day: Nine 7th- or 8th-level spells
3/Day: Six 9th-level spells
1/Day: A miracle (not governed by the limits of normal magic or spellcasting)

entries.name: Example (continued)
entries: Since Gheim is 2nd strata and uses the Trickery and Death domains, we'll have him prepare the following spells:
At-will: bane, blindness/deafness, charm person, command, false life, ray of sickness, silence, thaumaturgy
3/Day: blight, dispel magic, vampiric touch, banishment, geas, scrying
1/Day: dominate person, modify memory, cloudkill

entries.name: 6. Add Standard Divine Features
entries: Next, we'll add a few features that are standard for all gods in Planegea. Many high-level monsters will already have the Legendary Resistance feature, but if not, add it as well:
Legendary Resistance (3/Day). If the god fails a saving throw, it can choose to succeed instead.

Gather Magic (Recharge 5–6). The god focuses on their connection to the world's magic. If they have cast any spells with a per-day limit, the limit is reset as if they had not cast any such spells yet. When using this action, they can also exchange prepared spells for other known spells.

Spiritual Form. When the god would be reduced to 0 hit points, they are instead reduced to 1 hit point. They gain a number of temporary hit points equal to half of their maximum hit points and end all conditions affecting them.

They then take on their Spiritual Form, and their features and actions change accordingly. They retain their hit dice and hit point maximum, and features with limited uses that are present across multiple forms—such as Legendary Resistance, do not regain spent uses. While in their Spiritual Form, the god cannot take Lair Actions.

entries.name: Example (continued)
entries: Surprisingly, the Mummy Lord doesn't have Legendary Resistance, so we'll add that to Gheim along with Gather Magic and Spiritual Form!

entries.name: 7. Adjust Attacks & Features
entries: Now that you have your god mostly built, it can be helpful to look through its features and attacks to ensure they fit your concept well. Here are three tools for aligning anything that's missing, from simplest to most complex:

Reflavor. Ideally, you'll be able to make your god feel unique just by adjusting the language or flavor of what its attacks and features can do. This might include changing damage type (say, from cold to radiant), or simply expressing the exact same mechanical effect through a different visual experience. Reflavoring is best whenever you can get away with it, as you know it won't affect the final creature's balance too much.

Borrow. If you can't find the feature or attack you want, look at other monsters of the same CR or the same creature type and borrow their abilities. This may require some careful examination of where their numbers are coming from (if it's a melee attack, do the Strength scores between the original monster and your new stat block match?), but borrowing is relatively safe, as 5E mechanics are carefully constructed for game balance.

Invent. If you're careful and confident, you can invent wholly new features and attacks from scratch. This can be lots of fun and make your god feel wholly unique—but it also runs the risk of seriously challenging your players to a potentially unwinnable fight. Do this thoughtfully and rarely, unless it's something you're comfortable with.

entries.name: Damage by Strata
entries: When deciding on average damage per round, consider the god's other capabilities. Does it boast a higher or lower AC? Are they especially tough or fragile? Do their attacks and effects debilitate their enemies? If a god is weaker in other categories, then the damage should be on the upper end of the range. If it's stronger, the damage should probably be on the lower end of the range or right in the middle:

items: 1st strata: 60–99
2nd strata: 100–140
3rd strata: 141–230
4th strata: 231–320

entries: If your god has legendary actions that allow it to make attacks (most do), this should have an impact on the calculated damage of the god's attacks.

entries.name: Example (continued)
entries: Looking through the Mummy Lord's features one at a time, we'll make Rejuvenation refer to its divine ivory, turn its Rotting Fist into a Shadow Fist, and keep its Dreadful Glare exactly as it is.

Under legendary actions, we'll call Blinding Dust “Swirling Shadow,” but keep the mechanics the same. Let's make Blasphemous Word target non-fiendish creatures, leave Channel Negative Energy the same, and make Whirlwind of Sand into “Living Darkness.”
And just like that, with a few simple name changes and one minor targeting adjustment, we've turned our Mummy Lord into a living shadow god!

entries.name: 8. Create Lair Actions
entries: Referring back to your hallow sketch from Step 1, add three lair actions. If the base creature already has lair actions, you can choose your two favorites and adjust those just like the tweaks you made in Step 6. But all gods need to have the Visitants lair action:

Visitants. The god destroys and creates visitants. The visitants may only have the same creature type as their god, and the combined challenge rating of all visitants cannot exceed the strata's maximum visitant CR: 1st strata, 2; 2nd strata, 6; 3rd strata, 12; 4th strata, 18. (CR 0 visitants count as ¼ for this calculation.) Newly created visitants appear at locations of the god's choosing within their hallow and immediately roll initiative. Any visitants the god destroys vanish. All effects maintained by destroyed visitants instantaneously end.

Control. For one of your god's lair actions, choose an effect that allows it to move things or creatures around the battlefield. This action might not cause direct harm to the characters, but enables the god to take control of a fight that's not going their way by shifting the field of battle. Examples might be creating blinding effects, teleporting creatures, teleporting the god, or creating difficult terrain.

Offense. For the final lair action, create an offensive action that directly deals damage to players who fail a saving throw. Many lair actions in 5E fit this model. Skim through high-CR creatures in lairs to get an idea of the kind of offensive action that might fit your god.

entries.name: Example (continued)
entries: We'll give Gheim Shadow-Whisper the Visitants lair action, obviously. As for its other two actions, we'll give Gheim an action that allows him to switch the locations of two creatures (including himself) in the darkness, and one based on the idea of Maddening Whispers that deals 3d6 psychic damage to creatures who fail a saving throw versus his spell save DC.

entries.name: 9. Create Its Spiritual Form
entries: All gods have the Spiritual Form feature, which—when they would reach 0 hit points—unleashes their hidden spiritual nature into its final death throes, which are incredibly dangerous for the PCs. This form replaces all features and attacks with the following:

Thematic transformation. Consider how you want to flavor the god's transformation into this, their final form. It should be an awe-inspiring moment, and may reshape the lair around the god for a last round of combat.
Size increase. Usually, the spiritual form is at least 1 size category bigger than the god's physical form.
Creature type change. Some gods may change creature types when they transform, becoming more like an elemental, monstrosity, etc. Adjust their type as you see fit, but keep the (god) tag.

End spellcasting, lair actions, and visitants. Gods in their spiritual forms are creatures of pure emotion and instinct, and cannot cast complex spells or command their lairs. All visitants are destroyed as the god sucks all of its magic back into itself for a final onslaught.

Nova attacks. Using the god's spiritual form for inspiration, add an attack meant to be used in a large burst, harming the most creatures it can. Creatures within range should make a saving throw versus the god's spell save DC. On a failure, they should take damage of a type you choose, using the Damage By Strata sidebar. Difficulty in 5E is generally balanced around a party of four player characters, so a safe bet when calculating damage for nova effects is to take the expected damage per round and divide by four. Creatures who fail the saving throw may also suffer additional effects, such as being knocked prone, briefly blinded, or restrained. On a success, they should take half damage, and not suffer an additional effect.

Slaughter attacks. If there's one character that has sufficiently enraged a god, it may only want to target that character. Give your god a slaughter attack, where it's targeting a specific creature. Its attack roll will be based on an ability score that you choose + its proficiency bonus, and its damage on a hit should be of a type you choose, using the Damage By Strata sidebar. When the god takes the attack action, the action's total damage should fall within the expected damage range. The target may suffer other harmful effects on a hit, and the god may also do splash damage to other creatures.

Deadly presence. Consider whether to give your god a harmful effect simply when moving through or occupying another creature's space. The damage for this effect should be low, but now that the god is unbound and unpredictable, even standing near it might be dangerous.
Legendary Resistance. The spiritual form retains any remaining uses of Legendary Resistance the god has not yet used.

Other attacks and features. Since the spiritual form is so brief, usually only lasting a single round, it's okay to throw some experimental attacks and features into this moment. Be careful not to overload your god—they'll probably not survive long enough to use too many abilities. But having some fun with the final moment of your god's existence will make their death stick in the minds of your players long after the battle ends.

entries.name: Example (continued)
entries: Let's say that when Gheim is finally dealt the killing blow, the shadow drops away and all that's left is the madness. To disorient our players, we'll have him turn into a chaotic swirl of lights, formed of colors no mortal eye has ever seen or imagined. He'll go from Large to Gargantuan and fill his hallow, occupying the same space as all the PCs. We'll keep the Fiend creature type, and drop spellcasting, lair actions, and all visitants.

We'll add the following three abilities: For his nova attack, we'll say that he can deal psychic damage to all players in his space (which is everyone). For his Deadly Presence, we'll say he can try to shatter an individual mind with insane screams. We've already dealt with his Deadly Presence in the nova attack, but just to make things interesting, we'll add a final feature that says that as a legendary action, he can cause a player to make a Wisdom saving throw versus his spell save DC or attack the nearest friendly creature, chosen randomly.
And just like that, Gheim Shadow-Whisper is complete, and ready to menace our characters from the gloom of his deadly cavern!

entries.name: Creating a Substrata God
entries: If you want to build a god below CR 11, choose a creature to base it on. Adjust its alignment and creature type, giving it the “god” creature type tag. Give it the basic divine traits and increase its Charisma up to 12 (if needed). Give it innate spellcasting, with 3/Day 1st-level spells and a 1/day 2nd level spell.

Give it Legendary Resistance and Gather Magic, but not Spiritual Form. Leave all attacks and features as they are. Grant it a hallow (no larger than 25 ft. in diameter), and allow it to create up to 12 visitants with a combined CR of no more than 1. It does not have regional effects.

entries.name: Gods of Planegea

entries: To fight a god is to know true fear. The divinities of Planegea, although they are not the cosmos-spanning entities of later epochs, are beings of immense magical and physical power, and are always encountered within their hallows, which bend and warp reality itself to the will of the god.

The gods are as varied as the land and the creatures that inhabit it. And any single god can present many faces—one to its worshipers and another to its enemies. But gods are always powerful, and should not be lightly stirred to wrath.

entries.name: Divine Forms
entries: These pages describe two forms of each god. In truth, a god can have many forms, but these represent the main ways they express themselves in their hallows.

Physical Form. A god's physical form is the face they show to mortals. This form usually resembles their pre-divine nature—although they are almost always larger and more wondrous than before they ascended to godhood.

Spiritual Form. Glimpsing a god's spiritual form is the rarest and most awe-inspiring of experiences. When a god is brought low by sword or spell, its very being begins to splinter and break apart, and the magic that bound it together unravels into a final expression of power. The spiritual form is the god's essence made manifest, and it is terrible to behold—and difficult to survive.

entries.name: Divine Strata
entries: The shamans of Seerfall have roughly classified the known gods into four categories called strata. These are not strict categories, and gods move through them fluidly as they gain and lose glory—yet they are useful for discussing the relative might and power of divine beings.

First Strata. Gods of the 1st strata are demigods, able to act only as far as they can physically see, with limited spells and relatively weak visitants (created servants). Even a god of the 1st strata is a deadly challenge to most mortals, but these are usually gods who have either only recently manifested their divinity, or who have waned after a long lack of worship into a shadow of their former selves.

Second Strata. The 2nd strata is perhaps the most common classification of gods. Deities of the 2nd strata have powerful magic at their command, and can create daunting visitants. They rule local areas such as particular forests, valleys, or mountains, and are honored by a sufficient stream of glory from mortals. Some gods are content to remain in the 2nd strata. Indeed rising above it is difficult, requiring great devotion from followers, such that only a few gods have achieved it.

Third Strata. Few indeed are the gods of the 3rd strata. In fact, only four are commonly known: the Brothers (Urhosh, Kho, and Glelh) and their southern sister, Twr the Tyrant. These gods have influence that stretches across an entire region, and their visitants are legendary in their might. 3rd-strata gods are capable of the most powerful magic known to mortals, and once a god ascends to this level, it is almost unthinkable that they should descend to a lower order of being.

Fourth Strata. The 4th strata is practically theoretical. Only one god could claim to even approach this strata—Mala Long-Song, whale god of Scattersea. A 4th-strata god would have power to work wonders indescribable, bring forth visitants of titanic might, and could stretch their influence across the surface of Planegea. But even a god of the 4th strata would be confined to their hallow, and ultimately capable of death or descent, given sufficient loss of health or glory.

entries.name: Divine Alignment
entries: A god's alignment is both a description and a definition. Most gods do not enter the world with a particular alignment—it's far more common to find a neutral god than one which is particularly evil or good. But as glory accrues, gods of higher strata are more likely to take on evil or good qualities, depending on the nature of their worship and followers.

Evil. Evil gods are those whose worship takes the form of wicked rites and dark devotion. Such gods take on the fiend creature type, and can become either chaotic demons or hierarchical devils. Once a god has become evil, its redemption is almost impossible, as violence begets violence and its legacy is saturated with fear.

Neutral. Neutrality is the natural alignment of most deities, for they are not particularly concerned with good or evil, but rather focused on survival, like most creatures in Planegea. Neutral gods may favor order or chaos, but generally are not devoted to grand principles of any kind. Their creature type is tied to their pre-divine origin.

Good. Good gods are beloved by their followers, and exercise their power for blessing and justice. Such honorable beings acquire the celestial creature type. A good god is still unpredictable—such beings are good, but not tame—and good gods can prefer law or chance, depending on their personality and history. Happy are the people who serve a celestial… but beware, for in a world of violence and danger, even a good god can be tempted to rage and wrath.

entries.name: An Aside
entries: I have spent more years than can be named on Planegea fighting against the dark gods, beating them back and binding their powers. Without those of us who oppose them, surely their chaos and cruel laws would blot out the very Day-Star's light in a matter of a few short years.

entries.name: Glelh the Unblinking
entries: Deep in the hill country of the Lion Clan, a circle of stones juts from atop a steep rise. The hill is decorated with colored paints and powders, fluttering flags and feathers, and carved chimes that rattle in the wind. This is the hill-hallow of Glelh the Unblinking, god of the Lion Clan.

Those who are bidden to approach Glelh describe first and foremost, his eyes—unwavering amber depths, like staring into the Day-Star. Those eyes transfix the weak and the mighty, and sink into the soul like claws. Glelh takes the form of a mighty lion, long saber-teeth bared at any sign of disrespect or folly, yellow mane dusted with the powders of sacred pigments brought as offerings by the hypnotized shamans. Glelh is almost never seen outside his circle of stones, from which it is said that he watches the whole world, seeing all in stony silence.

Beauty & serenity. Glelh is best pleased with that which is pleasant to gaze upon, and his worshipers fashion bright colors and beautiful patterns to honor him. He is unforgiving towards weakness, believing that those who are worthy will endure even terrible suffering, and his rites can be truly cruel as they weed the weak from the stoic. But to those in whom he delights, Glelh gives great visions of far places, enabling glimpses into the very edges of reality and beyond.

The Feast of Longest Day. Glelh's high holy day is the Feast of Longest Day, in the heart of summer. On the day when there is the most sun to behold color and light and beauty, the Lion Clan engages in many ceremonies and artistic celebrations and voyages deep into the far places of the mind's eye on vision-walks. On the Feast of Longest Day, Glelh invests nearly all his power into visitants in the form of powerful lions. They walk among the people, beholding their artwork closely and testing their fortitude with deadly glares and radiant claws.

Piercing eyes, rending claws. Glelh's hypnotic power is perhaps most dangerous when causing one to forget that, in his heart, Glelh is a ravenous beast. The hilltop of his hallow is stained red with the blood of prey drawn from afar—even though as a god he has no physical hunger. His claws and teeth are terrible, and his might is immense when he is aroused to anger—as is his roar, which is said to only be heard once in a generation.

entries.name: Glelh's Hallow
entries: As a 3rd-strata god, Glelh has influence over an entire region. The epicenter of his power is his stone-encircled hilltop. The entire hill, however, is the hallow of Glelh, and a creature who steps foot onto that hill immediately feels an overwhelming sense of being fully seen and fully known, and judged guilty or favored in a moment.

The hilltop is strewn with colored art pieces of all sorts, some of which conceal cleverly-constructed traps and tricks hidden by shamans to protect their god against fools and the unfaithful. Its top is slick with the blood that coats the base of the standing stones, where Glelh feasts in the darkness, his eyes all that can be seen in the shadows.

entries.name: Lair Actions
entries: On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), Glelh takes a lair action to cause one of the following effects:

items: Glelh creates visitants. His visitants may only have a combined challenge rating of CR 12 or less. Newly created visitants appear at locations of Glelh's choosing within his hallow and immediately roll initiative. Visitants remain until they die, Glelh chooses to dismiss them (no action required), or Glelh's spiritual form activates. All effects maintained by visitants end when they disappear.

A beam of blinding light cast by a celestial body (Day-Star during day, moon during night) illuminates a 20-foot-radius, 60-foot-high cylinder within 120 feet of Glelh (and in his line of sight). All creatures in that area must make a DC 18 Constitution saving throw, becoming blinded on a failure. A blinded creature can repeat the saving throw at the end of subsequent turns, ending the effect on a success. The effect also ends if Glelh uses this lair action again or when Glelh enters his spiritual form.

A pride of celestial lions briefly apparate and attack creatures in a 20-foot-square centered on a point Glelh can see within 120 feet. The lions attack each creature of Glelh's choice in the area with a +10 bonus to hit, dealing 10 (3d6) slashing damage and 10 (3d6) radiant damage on a hit.

entries.name: Regional Effects
entries: Throughout the hilly domain of the Lion Clan, Glelh's influence can be clearly felt.

items: People within his reach are inspired to create art, infatuated with color and pattern. Visions of great works of art often appear in dreams, stirring those who have such dreams to throw themselves into trying to recreate what they have seen.
Aside from music or the jangling of wind chimes, the land is quiet. The DC for Wisdom (Perception) checks made with hearing is decreased by 2.

Glelh sees all in his domain unless creatures make a DC 20 Wisdom saving throw to shake off his vision. If creatures he can see act in a way that displeases him, he sends word to his shamans or visitants, and 1d6 guards or 1 visitant arrives to punish wrongdoing in 1d4–1 hours after a crime against his will is committed.

entries: If Glelh dies, these effects end in 2d4 weeks.

entries.name: Mala Long-Song
entries: Planegea's most powerful god may also be its most elusive. Mala Long-Song, deity of the Whale Clan, dwells at the very edge of the mortal world, at the far reaches of Scattersea. There, from her underwater hallow, she watches over all the islands in her vast domain, and she is worshiped by landfolk and merfolk alike.

Mala is an ancient whale-goddess, the size of an island. She ascends to the surface only once a year for air, and her ascension marks the holiest day in all of Scattersea. At all other times, she remains far below the waters, in her cylindrical hallow of light and song. Her skin is patterned with barnacles and scars that form graceful whirls and swirls, reminiscent of wave patterns and the tides.

Firstborn. Some say Mala Long-Song was the first god that mortals ever knew, in days nearly forgotten, when the sea was almost all there was. Mala protected the young clans as they tried to cling to life on the surface of the waters, and was repaid with gratitude and reverence. Her songs and island shrines have made her name great over the generations, and now there is no known god on land or sea who exceeds her in glory.

Above & Below. Mala is worshiped not only by the mortals who sail in canoes and catamarans, dwelling in coves and caves, but also by the merfolk below. These bring their offerings from the bounty of the sea, and news of the movements of the deep. The merfolk are proud and quarrelsome, and have little to do with the “dry people,” except for their high shaman, who by law is wedded to the high shaman from the world above in united worship of Mala.

The Day of Breath. Once a year, Mala rises from her swirling depths in a shining beam of light to breach the surface of the waters. Merfolk sing as she swims, and every canoe and catamaran in Scattersea gathers to hail her ascent on the Day of Breath. The moments she spends above the water are most mortals' only glimpse of the great goddess, and it's said that she blesses all who share in the one great breath that sustains her for the year to come.

Long Reach. Of all the gods in Planegea, only Mala is able to see across the entire world, and exert her will even in distant and dry places where other gods hold sway. Yet Mala is patient and wise, and has not lived as long as she has by encroaching on others' domains. Mala is slow to act, preferring to allow her followers to learn wisdom over their lifetimes and pass along their knowledge from parent to child. But when she is stirred to action, the song of her magic can pierce rock and bone, air and fire and shadow.

entries.name: Mala's Hallow
entries: The hallow of Mala Long-Song is a cylinder of water near the very last islands of Scattersea. These islands are sacred places, where only shamans and their chosen acolytes may travel, and where divine magic seeps from every stone and rises from every drop of water. The hallow stretches from the ocean's surface to an amphitheater of shells far below. Within the cylinder, shining lights from Mala's countless visitants circle and echo a never-ending song. It's said that those who hear Mala's choir hear the song of themselves, of all those who came before, and visions of what will come to pass in long years ahead.

entries.name: Lair Actions
entries: On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), Mala takes a lair action to cause one of the following effects:

items: Mala creates visitants. Her visitants may only have a combined challenge rating of CR 18 or less. Newly created visitants appear at locations of Mala's choosing within her hallow and immediately roll initiative. Visitants remain until they die, Mala chooses to dismiss them (no action required), or Mala's spiritual form activates. All effects maintained by visitants end when they disappear.

A swarming school of celestial fish appear in a 20-foot sphere centered on a point Mala can see within 120 feet. Creatures that start their turn in the area or move into it for the first time on a turn must make a DC 20 Wisdom saving throw or become stunned until the end of their turn. The school remains until Mala enters her spiritual form or Mala uses this lair action again.

Phantoms of the dead apparate and attack a creature that Mala can see within 120 feet. The creature must make a DC 20 Constitution saving throw, taking 42 (12d6) necrotic damage on a failure, or half as much on a success. Additionally, a creature that failed their saving throw begins drowning, provided it was holding its breath. The phantoms then disappear.

entries.name: Regional Effects
entries: Scattersea is full of Mala's magic, but it grows more intense closer to her hallow, near the Shaman Isles.

items: Floating song can be heard in the air, soothing emotion and giving creatures advantage on Wisdom (Insight) checks, Intelligence (History) checks, and Wisdom saving throws.
Groups of 2d6 merfolk travel through the water, bringing offerings on a pilgrimage to Mala's hallow, or returning home after an audience with the goddess.
Soft, white flowers appear from nowhere, blossoming in midair and drifting down to float on the ocean's surface. At night they glow with a gentle radiance, casting dim light in for 1 foot.
Creatures unable to breathe water are able to hold their breath for twice as long.

entries: If Mala dies, these effects end after 2d8 months.

entries.name: Kho Many-Arms
entries: The Oldest Brother. The Weary One. The Many-Armed Ape-God. Generous in his slumber and dangerous when he wakes, Kho is a difficult god with many names. The Ape Clan seeks to surround him in the drumbeats that soothe his mind, keeping him asleep—for in his sleep, he is kind.

Kho appears as a massive ape, covered in dark hair and amulets. True to his name, he has many arms—some physical, some spiritual, formed of golden light. He moves slowly, unless he is aroused to wrath. As long as the drums thunder and glory flows towards him, Kho can be seen resting in the branches of his gargantuan tree-hallow, slumbering the seasons away as he dreams of long ago.

Ancient torpor. Except for Mala Long-Song, Kho is the most ancient god in Planegea. His years have wearied him, and he takes no pleasure in the gone-in-a-blink concerns of mortals. Kho wants nothing more than to sleep, and the Ape Clan scurries to serve his unconscious will, working magic to bind him in his dreams and holding their breath at the least sign of his awakening.

Dreams of youth. Kho dreams of the living forests of his youth, when he was a mere apeling, leaping through high branches without worry or power. His dreams are full of fun, adventure, and good spirits. Because of his magic, his dreams are often made manifest, and those who approach his tree-hallow can be caught up in them, carried into the past with their god on the slippery surface of time. Kho can bring objects from the past of his dreams into the present, and—if he is pleased—make almost anything real.

Wrathful waking. When the drumming stops, or his dream is interrupted, Kho descends from his tree in dark spirits. Those within reach of his power tremble, for his waking is full of unbridled rage at time and the world. Each year, when the Ape Clan travels through the passages of Nod to Wintersouth, the year's worst criminal is left behind as punishment, the last person banging the drum to keep Kho asleep. And though they try to bang on the drum as long as they can, eventually they tire and the beat falters—and then the titanic tree begins to shake with the rage of the waking god…

entries.name: Kho's Hallow
entries: Kho sleeps in a gargantuan tree, as ancient as himself, whose roots are so deep and so powerfully thirsty that they bend the Ape River towards it. The tree is a landmark that can be seen for miles. Its lowest branches are filled with gifts and toys that dangle from leather straps, which Kho's followers toss up from below as offerings. Every now and then, Kho will stir, and a toy will catch his eye—a massive finger will reach out from the tree, touch the toy, and the god will go back to sleep again, mollified for the moment.

The tree and the ground surrounding its base are the hallow of Kho, and to enter his hallow is to step inside either his dreamworld or the epicenter of his waking wrath. As long as he sleeps, the hallow occupies a different time than the outer world—it may be day inside and night outside, or vice versa. Inside the dream, what mortals see can shift according to Kho's whim, but the great tree always remains at the center of the hallow, unchanged.

entries.name: Lair Actions
entries: On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), Kho takes a lair action to cause one of the following effects:

items: Kho creates visitants. His visitants may only have a combined challenge rating of CR 12 or less. Newly created visitants appear at locations of Kho's choosing within his hallow and immediately roll initiative. Visitants remain until they die, Kho chooses to dismiss them (no action required), or Kho's spiritual form activates. All effects maintained by visitants end when they disappear.

An arm formed from spirit energy appears at a point Kho can see on a surface within 120 feet. The arm has an AC of 16, 35 hit points, and shares Kho's damage resistances. When it appears and on each of Kho's turns, the arm makes a melee weapon attack against a creature of Kho's choice (+16 to hit, reach 30 ft., one target). On a hit, it deals 23 (4d6 + 9) force damage. The arm disappears if it is reduced to 0 hit points, if Kho uses this lair action again, or if Kho's spiritual form triggers.

Kho's hollow briefly shifts, merging the realms of the physical and dream worlds. Each creature in Kho's hollow must make a DC 18 Wisdom saving throw. On a failure, the creature is affected as by the confusion spell. If Kho uses this lair action again or enters his spiritual form, the effect ends on any affected creatures.

entries.name: Regional Effects
entries: Throughout Ape Clan territory, Kho's dreaming or waking is clearly felt, an ever-present reassurance or terrifying threat, depending on the mood of the god.

items: Families of apes wander freely, unchallenged and sacred to the Ape Clan. These apes are allowed to go where they please and take or eat whatever they wish.
A creature who presses their ear to the ground and succeeds on a DC 10 Wisdom (Perception) check can hear the faint rumble of Kho's sleeping breath.

Doorways to Nod are commonplace while Kho sleeps. In places of shadow or mist, a doorway can be found on a successful DC 18 Intelligence (Investigation) check. When a door is found, roll 1d20. On a 5 or lower, the door opens to the Nightmare World. On a 6 or higher, the door opens to the Dream World.

Creatures and places glimmer and change slightly according to Kho's dreams. While his dreams are pleasant, translucent flowers and fey beings can be briefly glimpsed, but vanish if approached. If Kho's dreams are troubled, shadowy forms of malice flicker at the edges of vision, disappearing when examined.

When Kho awakes, all other regional effects end (except for the wandering apes), and the land shakes in an earthquake. Creatures must succeed on a DC 12 Dexterity saving throw or be knocked prone, and all structures take 10 points of bludgeoning damage.

entries: If Kho dies, these effects end in 2d4 weeks.

entries.name: Twr the Tyrant
entries: The winter gods are violent and chaotic, thriving on the fear and misery of mortals, drawing their glory from the desperation that comes at the edge of death. Of all these dark deities, one stands head and shoulders above the rest—Twr the Tyrant, a powerful goddess of fire and water, ruler of the Ox River and dread power of the Wintersouth.

Cult paintings of Twr depict her as a towering female form with sweeping, batlike wings, clad in shadow, fire, smoke, and steam. She has long, sharp horns like an ox, and her mouth, eyes, and skull-like nostrils burn with shimmering red flame. Twr wears bone armor, cracked and blackened from the heat of her blazing body, and her symbol is a flaming whip and a raging bull.

The Boiling River. Twr is a goddess of flame, bound to the Ox River and the arid Wintersouth. It is said that long ago a frenzied she-ox was bound to a rock by the river, and so great was her fury that she survived on rage alone, boiling the water with the heat of her anger. Over time, the legend grew, the magic of the world pooled with the fear of mortals, and Twr took form as a goddess. Her hallow is a fork in the river, wrapped in perpetual darkness, where the water scalds any who touches it. For miles in each direction, the water bubbles and steams, one of the only reliable sources of heat in all of the wintry lands that surround it.

Sister Cults. The Brothers of the Great Valley—the Bear, Ape, and Lion gods and their clans—are rivaled in power only by Twr, goddess of the third strata, who could look any of them in the eye and laugh in defiance. Calling herself the Sister, Twr exerts great influence over the clans who travel south to escape the Great Valley's harsh winter. Some clans see her as their primary god, since it is by her whim and craving for power that they are allowed to live under her shadow for the long winter months. These Ox Clans or Sister Cults dwell by the boiling river and serve Twr's will in a hallowcamp built of skulls and black pelts.

Ivory Eater. Since early in her divinity, Twr has aggressively sought out the divine ivory of other gods, sending her best warriors and most powerful visitants to assault weak winter gods in nearby hallows. Her hunger for ivory is nearly as endless as her hunger for glory. When a god devours divine ivory, they take on aspects of the god they consume, and the merging of the forms occurs in such a way as to make the god take on a slightly more humanoid or giantish form. Twr has eaten the divine ivory of a great bat and gained spreading wings, as well as the divine ivory of a lizard god, granting her clawed hands and a long, lashing tail, Twr stands upright, like a giant she-minotaur, and sends out her worshipers for more of the ivory she craves.

Endless Menace. Twr's pride and tyranny are a never-ending threat to the greater powers of the region. Her claws and ambitions stretch northward towards Edgegather, and southward towards Seerfall. Sister Cults erect shrines and propagate her worship in those cities, which are quickly torn down by those opposed to her worship. It is not uncommon for her to send a violent visitant into the midst of mortals to inspire terror and awe.

entries.name: Twr's Hallow
entries: There is a place where the Ox River splits and where shadows grow to blot out the sun. There is no sound of bird or beast, only the bubbling roar of the boiling river and the crackle of fire as it burns on the shore. This steaming darkness is the Hallow of Twr. It is a terrifying place, unsuitable for any but her shamans, who are granted protection if they please her, and searing death if they do not.

entries.name: Lair Actions
entries: On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), Twr takes a lair action to cause one of the following effects:

items: Twr creates visitants. Her visitants may only have a combined challenge rating of CR 12 or less. Newly created visitants appear at locations of Twr's choosing within her hollow and immediately roll initiative. Visitants remain until they die, Twr chooses to dismiss them (no action required), or Twr's spiritual form activates. All effects maintained by visitants end when they disappear.

A 30-foot-radius cloud of steam rises from the surface of Twr's river hollow, centered on a point Twr can see within 90 feet. The cloud's area is heavily obscured for all creatures except Twr, and when a creature starts their turn within or enters the cloud's area, they must make a DC 18 Constitution saving throw or take 10 (3d6) fire damage. The cloud remains until it is dispersed, Twr uses this lair action again, or her spiritual form triggers.

Mud superheats and hardens in a 20-foot square centered on a point on the ground that Twr can see within 120 feet. All creatures in the area must make a DC 18 Dexterity saving throw or be restrained by hardened mud. At the start of their turn, a restrained creature takes 7 (2d6) fire damage. A restrained creature can be freed if it or another creature takes an action to make a DC 18 Strength check. If Twr uses this lair action again or her spiritual form triggers, the mud liquifies, releasing any trapped creatures.

entries.name: Regional Effects
entries: A wide region surrounding the Ox River is under the sway of Twr the Tyrant. Travelers through her lands should be cautious, for her wrath simmers just out of sight…

items: Steam rises from underground pools, drawing creatures seeking relief from the frigid winter nights. The steam scalds any who touch it, dealing 1d6 fire damage on a failed DC 14 Dexterity saving throw.
Roaming visitants threaten and harass mortals. Every 24 hours, roll 1d20. On a 5 or lower, 1d6 quasits attempt to intimidate the characters in the name of Twr.

Domesticated beasts turn bad-tempered, and are liable to injure their masters and break free of their bonds. Freed beasts have a 50% chance of attacking their former masters, otherwise they seek to escape into the wild.
Those who sleep have dreams about drowning in scalding water or being lost in fiery darkness without end.

entries: If Twr dies, these effects end in 2d4 weeks.

entries.name: Unkillable Urhosh
entries: Excess in all things is the mark of Unkillable Urhosh, cave-bear god of the Brothers. An unstoppable force of life, Urhosh began his existence long ago as a massive cave bear whom no hunter, predator, or natural disaster could topple—and over the years, as tales of his hardiness grew, so did the respect and reverence of the mortals of the region, who started to swear by Urhosh's name and call upon his power.

Urhosh is a huge, shaggy brown cave bear. His back and flanks bristle with snapped spears and arrows, fired by hunters from years ago, which have stuck into his matted pelt, but that he never even felt. His eyes glow with white light, and he seems to feel neither pain nor heat nor cold.

Lord of Feasts & Honey. Urhosh is a god of plenty, providing bounty for his people and expecting bounty in return. He is most pleased by great feasts held at the mouth of his cave-hallow, and blesses his followers with friendly beehives and powerful magic. From revelry to wrestling, boasting matches to bold raids on the clan's enemies, Urhosh's clan pour their hearts into all they do.

Fearless Immortality. On the rare occasions where Urhosh is stirred to violence, there is no subtlety in his approach. He is a god who has lived all his days unable to die, and he charges into combat without the slightest fear of pain or defeat. Urhosh knows that he will live, and grants that confidence and life force to his hunters and warriors, preparing them to rush headlong into danger just as he does.

Winter's Rest. When the Bear Clan departs southward on the Winterwalk, the gregarious Urhosh retreats to his cave to hibernate. For many years, his high shaman—the tiny, white-haired Lak'hayel—has entered the hallow with him at autumn's end to great ceremony. There she falls into a magical slumber and rests by the side of her god until spring comes, and the returning clan reawakens both god and high shaman.

entries.name: Urhosh's Hallow
entries: The hallow of Urhosh is a deep cave set into the side of a rocky hill, as well as its threshold, where a great stone feasting table has been set up for time immemorial, and dozens of hives of honeybees buzz their endless honeysong. Entering Urhosh's hallow bestows a feeling of deep wellbeing, of bold recklessness and invincibility. His cave descends deep into the earth, and Urhosh has many curiosities of his long life hidden far within its recesses. Few ever pass beyond the threshold, though, and Urhosh is usually eager to come out and spend time with his people as they feast in his presence.

entries.name: Lair Actions
entries: On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), Urhosh takes a lair action to cause one of the following effects. The god can't use the same effect two rounds in a row:

items: Urhosh creates visitants. His visitants may only have a combined challenge rating of CR 12 or less. Newly created visitants appear at locations of Urhosh's choosing within his hollow and immediately roll initiative. Visitants remain until they die, Urhosh chooses to dismiss them (no action required) or Urhosh's spiritual form activates. All effects maintained by visitants end when they disappear.

A wave of protective magic washes through Urhosh's hollow. All creatures of Urhosh's choice gain 24 (4d8 + 6) temporary hit points. These temporary hit points remain until they are depleted, until Urhosh uses this lair action again, or until Urhosh's spiritual form activates.

Visions of a grand feast that occupy a 5-foot cube appear centered on a point that Urhosh can see within 60 feet. All creatures within 30 feet of that point must make a DC 18 Wisdom saving throw or become charmed and use their movement on each of their turns to move as close as possible to the illusory feast. Creatures can repeat the Wisdom saving throw before they would pass through obviously harmful terrain, when they take damage, or when they end their turn within 5 feet of the illusion. If Urhosh deals damage to a charmed creature, the effect on them ends. The illusory feast lasts until Urhosh uses this lair action again or until Urhosh's spiritual form activates.

entries.name: Regional Effects
entries: Urhosh's power extends far and wide, thanks to the boldness of the Bear Clan in building his hive shrines for miles in every direction.

items: Honeybees swarm plentifully, providing natural bounty everywhere. A creature that succeeds on a DC 10 Wisdom (Survival) check can find a nearby hive with fresh honeycomb.
Wild vegetables grow doubly large here, infused by the lifegiving power of the god. Creatures who gather plant food collect twice as much as usual.
After three days in Urhosh's domain, a creature must make a DC 8 Wisdom saving throw. On a failure, they believe themselves invincible to pain or harm. The effect ends when they take damage.
When taking a short rest, creatures gain an additional 1d4 hit points.

entries: If Urhosh dies, these effects end in 2d4 weeks.

entries.name: Bestiary
entries: Now we come to a deadly span of pages—the details of the many beasts and monsters that roam the land, sea, and sky of Planegea. The creatures listed here are simply the very outer edge of all that exists in the world. All the beings of 5th Edition have their place in Planegea. But the ones described here are particular to this world.

entries.name: Arctusk (Deinotherium)
entries: Trees bend and creak as the mighty arctusk wraps its thick trunk around their branches, using its long curving tusks to strip them of leaves and bark. The arctusk looks like a tall, slender elephant, with small ears, a short, thick trunk, and two curving tusks that curve downward from its lower lip towards its heart.

Shearing Browser. A gentle but powerful beast, the arctusk is primarily interested in finding food. It grips tree branches with its trunk and then runs its downward-curving tusks along the branch and tree, covering the ground in plant matter. It then browses the sheared greenery at its leisure, and can be quite choosy about its favorite morsels. When trees are scarce, it uses its tusks to dig for roots and water, which is key to survival in the arid Wintersouth.

Common Ivory. The arctusks' ivory, though not as long or fine as that of a mammoth, is plentiful, since the arctusks are able to regrow lost or broken tusks over time. An arctusk sighting often prompts a clan to send gatherers out to follow it at a distance, watching for dropped ivory in its path, which can be of great use in crafting and building. Some clans have successfully domesticated arctusks, who make excellent companions for most of the year—although the clan must be careful to turn them loose and stay far away during musk season.

Musk Season. The normally docile arctusk is harmless enough until late spring—also known as musk season. When the males are driven to mate, they exude a powerful, reeking musk, which hangs in the air in a stinking cloud and can be smelled for miles. During this time, they are belligerent, even frenzied, and use shows of aggression to attract their mates. Many clans have been scattered by the mad, stabbing and stomping onslaught of a musking arctusk.

Hated by Treants. Though treants and other awakened plants have little patience for large herbivores at the best of times, they reserve a special hatred for arctusks, whose hunger can leave an entire grove stripped and bare in a matter of hours. If treants become aware of an arctusk in the area, whether wild or domesticated, they will avoid it or kill it before it damages them and their slumbering brethren.

entries.name: Batface
entries: Many hapless wanderers, taking shelter for the night in a cave or under cliffs, have woken to find themselves silently surrounded by scores of glittering eyes. Bipedal mouselike creatures, covered in sleek gray fur with a white ruff at their necks, are the last beings their victims ever see. Black, pushed-in faces with enormous ears at first give a sweet, if eerie impression—and then the batfaces bite with their needle-like teeth.

The bite of a batface has a natural toxin that numbs, charms, and induces drowsiness. Bitten creatures lose consciousness quickly, and their last thought is euphoric—they feel that the world is kind and beautiful; it's good that they can provide food for these adorable, noble creatures.

Silent Crag-Dwellers. Batfaces make their homes among rocks, in tribes of about fifty, ruled by a pair of matriarchs. They fashion primitive nets and ropes and shabby shelters made from the gear and skin of their victims, and live in silent squalor, using gestures with their tiny, nimble hands to communicate. When not hunting or expanding their settlement, batfaces use stones to make countless tiny scratches on the wall, like prisoners counting the days of their confinement. Nobody knows the significance of these marks except the batfaces, but learned travelers know to avoid caves with such marks.

Creeping Threat. Batfaces are carnivores who never attack directly and scatter if threatened, subsisting on insects when larger beasts are scarce. Their chief method of securing prey is to find a creature sleeping within their territory, surround them noiselessly, and secure them gently with ropes and nets. Once satisfied that their prey is trapped, they begin to bite. The bite usually wakes up the ensnared creature, but by then it's already too late.

Valued Toxin. Batfaces meet their stealthy match in halflings, who know their ways well, and have spent many long nights struggling against the little creatures in silent darkness. Halflings hunt them for their toxin, which they apply to their weapons. It can also be distilled into a powerful, euphoric liquor called smiledrink.

entries.name: Smiledrink
entries: Smiledrink is a thick, clear liquid with a pearlescent glitter, made from the toxin of the batface. A creature that consumes smiledrink becomes poisoned for 1 hour, during which time they experiences euphoria and powerful feelings of universal love and peace.

Any creature that consumes smiledrink has a 10 percent chance of falling unconscious for the duration of the drink's effect. When the creature awakes, it must succeed on a DC 15 Wisdom saving throw or be stunned for 1d4 hours. The stunned creature experiences feelings of deep longing, grief, and melancholy.

entries.name: Dinosaurs & Leatherbeasts
entries: The world of Planegea is rich with saurian life, but the dinosaurs and other huge reptiles called leatherbeasts—those great monsters of old bloodlines and long memories—are among the most powerful and ferocious.

Arising primarily from the Venom Abyss, these creatures come in myriad forms, with scales and feathers of every color. Although some have been domesticated, even tame dinosaurs and leatherbeasts are creatures of instinct, respecting no authority besides hunger.

entries.name: Bouldertail (Ankylosaurus)
entries: Though they may graze on bush and brush, the giant, armored bouldertail is to be feared. With bones like stone fused to their backs and bodies, and tails like war-clubs, they crush bone and cripple the legs of even the largest of monsters, smashing the chests of many who have tried to approach them.

Bouldertails are long-lived and fearsome, and a single tamed bouldertail can provide protection and muscle for a clan for generations. Human-led clans have been known to risk everything to capture and befriend a bouldertail, knowing that such thundering power on their side will be worth the cost of the lives lost in the attempt.

entries.name: Chattercrest (Caviramus)
entries: These gregarious little soarers are remarkable for their brightly colored crests and their ability to mimic even complex human speech patterns. Domesticated long ago, chattercrests can be trained to fly far-flung routes and deliver spoken or sung messages, making them invaluable messengers for mortal clans.

Edgegather is famous for its chattercrest aviaries, where obsessive collectors barter enormous deals for creatures with unusually spectacular frills. Here chattercrest races and singing competitions are popular occasions for entertainment and gambling.

entries.name: Deathwalker (Gigantosaurus)
entries: The horrors of the Nightmare World seem flimsy and frail when compared to the terror of the monster they call the deathwalker. A mountain of muscle and malice, with teeth like swords and all the raw power of the Venom Abyss in its roar, the deathwalker is a beast made of pure fear. Even the tyrantmaw flees like a frightened shrub-hen when the deathwalker bellows.
No deathwalker has ever been glimpsed outside of the Venom Abyss. If one was, it would certainly be worshiped immediately as a god—though such worship would scarcely save those it chose as prey.

entries.name: Doomclaw (Utahraptor)
entries: These feathered, sickle-clawed predators are feared throughout the Venom Abyss. With sleek bodies, beautiful plumage, and long tails, at first they appear as graceful jungle spirits. Their eyes sparkle with predatory cunning, and they speak in a rough language of their own (with some even grasping the basics of other tongues).

To see one doomclaw is to be hunted by at least two others, and those bright, bloodstained feathers are the last thing many hunters have ever seen. Doomclaws mark their territory with long gashes, gouged in trees and earth with their terrifying claws, and filled with the fresh blood of their victims, poured out like an offering to the dark spirits the doomclaws revere.

entries.name: Farloper (Gallimimus)
entries: These long-legged, ostrich-like dinosaurs are favorite steeds of plains-dwellers. They are bad-tempered and nervous, but intelligent, and can be tamed quickly if raised from the egg. A farloper can cover enormous distances quickly, and is strong enough to carry two or three riders at once.

In the wild, farlopers travel in massive herds, which can cover an entire plain in their numbers. A farloper stampede that fills the land with its drumming feet can cut off the route of travelers for days at a time.

entries.name: Flintback (Stegosaurus)
entries: This shimmering plant-eater is a sight to behold. Beloved by mortals across the Great Valley and considered good fortune, a herd of flintbacks is occasion for a clan to stop work for the day to draw near and gaze in wonder. Slow-moving and calm, flintbacks can ignite their large back plates with magical light, causing a sense of hope and euphoria in those who behold the sight.

From a distance, flintbacks are delightful, but the dazzling display is deadly up close. When predators or hunters attempt to harm a flintback, its magical gleam stops them in their tracks. This creates an opening in which its spiked tail can swing in and smash the would-be attacker while they stare at the beauty that kills them. Particularly strategic warlords have been known to mount elite hunters on enraged flintbacks, using their magic and might to disarm and destroy those who oppose them.

entries.name: Frilled Spitter (Dilophosaurus)
entries: Only a little larger than a dog, these lean therapods have intelligent eyes and long, delicate claws held close to their chests as if they are afraid to get them dirty. At first, they give a harmless and curious impression—they seem eager to get close to other animals, and have a playful and cheery air. That is, until they attack.

When they strike, the frilled spitter expands a bright, colorful neck-frill that rattles, and they emit a shrill hiss, like a cobra. The hiss is followed by an expulsion of an acidic venom that blinds and stings their prey, rendering them vulnerable to the frilled spitter's needle-sharp teeth.

Frilled spitters rarely make their way out of the Venom Abyss, but when they do, the Venomguard seeks to hunt them down as quickly as possible—they breed quickly and lay scores of eggs in a clutch, so a few frilled spitters on the loose can quickly become an epidemic.

entries.name: Great-Hook (Therizinosaurus)
entries: The towering great-hook looks like a gigantic cross between a sloth and a chicken, and would be comical if it weren't so staggeringly enormous. Its huge hooked claws lock onto the trunk of a tree while it uses its long neck to browse for delicate foliage and moss.

The great-hook moves slowly and deliberately, and even when threatened demonstrates a kind of slow-motion grace in its arced neck and extended claws. Great-hooks are among the only dinosaurs known to face down the tyrantmaw and live, their immense claws providing ample defense against even the most aggressive predator.

The enormous claws of the great-hook are highly sought by crafters, and many tribes have tried to domesticate the gentle titan. However, mortal clans seem to be beneath its notice, as it continues on its deliberate way, searching for the next leafy tree.

entries.name: Greatwing (Quetzalcoatlus)
entries: A huge, swiftly-moving shadow is the last thing one sees before the horror begins. The greatwing is the apex predator of the skies. A single greatwing can drop from above, stabbing with a beak longer than a man is tall, then chase down fleeing prey with a four-legged gallop before spearing it in the back. But greatwings hunt in flocks, not alone—and a handful of greatwings are more than enough to devastate a clan and terrify an entire region.

Those who understand the secret ways of greatwings know that they harbor a particular hatred for dragons, and will band together to drive out a dragon from their territory, even if doing so requires a suicidal onslaught.

entries.name: Littlesnap (Euparkaria)
entries: These miniature raptors developed strength not as individuals, but in numbers. Tiny creatures who look like no more than hatchlings, littlesnaps are jewel-colored, with oversized heads and feet.

Littlesnaps travel not in packs, but in swarms. Once they bring their prey to the ground, they can cover it entirely in their brightly-colored bodies, picking its skeleton clean in a matter of minutes.

entries.name: Madcrash (Yi Qi)
entries: These daredevil gliders are a joke at best and a terror at worst, hurtling with suicidal speed through the foliage of the primordial jungle. Awkward and clumsy, they lurch from branch to branch, crashing constantly as they scrabble and careen in chaotic zig-zag movements.

An angry madcrash is a dangerous enemy not because it is particularly powerful, but because it has no sense of self-preservation whatsoever, and will divebomb even mighty creatures with snapping jaws and scrabbling claws until it or its target drops dead.

entries.name: Mammothmaw
entries: This gargantuan dinosaur is mostly mouth, with a huge head like an alligator and a short, sluggish body. The mammothmaw is an ambush predator who rarely moves if it can help it. It finds a grove or canyon in which to wait, and remains perfectly still with its mouth hanging open. Once it lurches forward and snaps its jaw closed, it locks down with such a powerful bite that it's incapable of opening its mouth again for an hour.

Mammothmaws are patient, often allowing smaller beasts to scurry across their tongues, or even nest among their teeth. They can eat as infrequently as once every few months, provided they can bring their huge bite down on a sufficiently large victim.

entries.name: Sailstalker (Spinosaurus)
entries: The swamps and marshes are dangerous lands. The shallow water and tall bush hide a dangerous monster. Quiet, stalking—then suddenly, with a rush, it lunges and sinks its teeth into its prey, thrashing its muscular neck, its finned back flexing as it drowns its prey, then swallows it whole. Then, the sailstalker fades into the marshy edges, waiting for its next meal.

Sailstalkers are most commonly found in Ghostmire. There, the undeath of the place has seeped into their skin, and the membrane of their sails faintly glows green and rattles with the spirits of the creatures they have devoured. Some even say that it's possible to whisper to those long-dead through such a creature's sail—that the walls of death are thin around such beasts—if you could only approach one without joining the dead yourself.

entries.name: Other Dinosaurs
entries: The creatures listed here are only a handful of those that roam the world of Planegea, and are by no means an exhaustive list. Many 5th Edition materials list iconic beasts like those listed below—be sure to draw them from those sources as well for your games.

items: Beakchaser (Hadrosaurus)
Hornbeast (Triceratops)
Littleclaw (Deinonychus)
Razormaw (Allosaurus)
Sailcrawler (Dimetrodon)
Stompwalker (Brontosaurus)
Tyrantmaw (Tyrannosaurus Rex)
Wavesnake (Plesiosaurus)

entries.name: Seatooth (Mosasaurus)
entries: This massive ocean predator strikes fear into all who cross the open waters. One does not see it approach. It strikes without warning from deep underneath, its jaws hitting the side of the canoe like a great stone hammer. The seatooth rivals any of the great monsters of the land for pure malice and savagery. They are especially well-known for their cannibalistic ways, hunting each other with more hunger and hatred than any other prey.

entries.name: Skullsmasher (Pachycephalosaurus)
entries: A favorite companion of gatherers, the skullsmasher is a two-legged, beaked dinosaur with a thick, bony crown. Even-tempered and affectionate, the skullsmasher lives in harmony with many clans, hauling sledges and ramming trees to shake down ripe fruit.

Skullsmashers must be trained from a young age and bond to a single person. Their loyalty and enthusiasm can become dangerous if their bonded mortal is threatened, since an angry skullsmasher is capable of killing someone easily with their powerful ramming attack.

entries.name: Snakeneck (Elasmosaurus)
entries: These aquatic beasts lurk in deep water, their snakelike necks waving slowly, eyes scanning the patterns of light above. When they see a school of fish—or a small mortal—they rush upwards in a burst of speed, creating a vortex of water with their swirling fins while their heads burst out of the water, only to plunge down again in an attempt to swallow any smaller creature whole.

Generally considered an ill omen by fishers and seagoers, the snakeneck is particularly feared by halfling-kind, and many halfling fear-tales circle around the prowling snakeneck and its sudden, terrifying hunger.

entries.name: Spider Raptor
entries: Through a quirk of the laws that govern survival, a strange and unsettling breed of raptor has been sighted by the Venomguard. These creatures, called Spider Raptors, have elongated limbs—spindly, reaching arms and long legs suited to climbing, dangling, and springing great distances.

Spider raptors have been seen scrambling up the great cliffs that keep other creatures confined to the Venom Abyss, and are starting to nest in the swamps, forests, and rocky hills of the Great Valley, using stealth, speed, and their slender, agile claws to chase and hook unsuspecting prey in dark places.

entries.name: Swiftclaw (Velociraptor)
entries: With rustling hisses and squawking shrieks, a flock of filthy swiftclaws swarms unwitting prey. Shedding blood-streaked feathers as they leap, swiftclaws sink their talons into their quarry, then flap wildly to remain upright as they tear into the flesh of their bewildered meal. A flock of swiftclaws can devour a beast in a matter of minutes.
Hunters throughout Planegea know to avoid a certain bloody, birdlike stench, a scattering of brown and blue feathers, and a high, warbling call that sounds like wild, cursed laughter.

entries.name: Tentwing (Pteranodon)
entries: This flying leatherbeast is a familiar sight throughout Planegea. They are social creatures, hooting and gabbling at all hours of the day and night, spreading their wings wide in the daylight like sentinels to plunge after some fish or swimming lizard.

Because of the ubiquity of tentwings, some clans have managed to tame them, using them for hunting or scouting. Giants have been known to train them to land on their forearms, and to screech at the sight of mortals running from the raiding party.

entries.name: Thunderfoot (Titanosaurus)
entries: The greatest of all dinosaurs are hardly beasts at all—they are living mountains. They move through the world like an earthquake, and mortals are mere pebbles to their slow and majestic step. Forests and clans have been leveled by thunderfeet, without the creatures ever stopping to notice that they passed through trees or fire.

Thunderfeet are incredibly secretive about their births and deaths. If a calf is to be born or an elder is near the end of its life, a herd of thunderfeet will withdraw beyond the ability of any nearby creatures to follow, guarding the start and end of their herdmate's life with the jealous severity of titans.

entries.name: Trumpetcrest (Parasaurolophus)
entries: A bugling cry breaks out across the water, and a riot of color splashes the surface of the marsh. Trumpetcrests are well-loved beasts—beautiful, gentle, and playful, though none too intelligent and easily startled. Hunters traveling through wetlands must be careful not to alarm these creatures; one blast from their resounding cranial cavity and all wildlife within half a day's walk scatters.

Because of their beauty, they are rarely hunted, yet trumpetcrest skulls are prized for the fashioning of instruments. Often, young gatherers will dare each other to seek out skulls in the wetlands, and many a clan has lost members to those who ventured too deep into the wrong marsh for a trumpetcrest skull.

entries.name: Dire Sloth
entries: Gigantic, slow-moving, and gentle, a group of dire sloths is a common sight in grasslands, eternally shuffling forward, their conical muzzles browsing among tough grasses for their favorite morsels. When rearing up on their hind legs to scan the horizon, they stand as tall as a mammoth, with brown, shaggy coats, and a reputation for tranquility.

entries.name: Lazy Grazers
entries: Although many clans have successfully domesticated dire sloths by tempting them with their favorite tender grasses, the feat is almost never worth the effort, since dire sloths are impossible to motivate to do anything but shuffle forward and eat.

entries.name: Tight-Knit Families
