entries: Dire sloths live in groups of 3 to 6, which are usually a single family of a few generations. At a certain age, young males wander away to make their own families. Once formed, these family units are inseparable. When threatened, a family of dire sloths will stand back-to-back, relying on their size and their long curved claws to fight off any threat.

entries.name: Living Towers
entries: Dire sloths are so gentle and single-minded in their pursuit of food that many migrating clans use them as living lookout towers. A dire sloth, if approached carefully, does not mind the little commotion of mortals, and scouting parties will often scale a sloth, climbing onto its neck and tempting it to rear up on its hind legs with a handful of carefully chosen grass to allow a better view of the horizon.

entries.name: Pixie Playgrounds
entries: The gentle nature and sheer scale of dire sloths often attracts the attention of pixies, those curious, playful, and gentlest of the fey. A family of dire sloths often lives in symbiosis with an encampment of pixies, and it's not an uncommon site to see bobbing lights swirling around a family as they browse the leaves. The pixies use the dire sloths as a muscular shield from those who would hunt them for their dust, and the dire sloths are grateful for the pixie's ability to steer them clear of unnatural dangers beyond their slow-moving senses.

entries.name: Ghost Monkey
entries: Of the many horrors of the Venom Abyss, there is a strange place reserved for tales of ghost monkeys. These nattering, aggressive, arboreal spirits are enough to drive even a seasoned wanderer mad.

At first glance, these apparitions appear to be howler monkeys—small, with prehensile tails and dirty reddish fur with shaggy ruffs around their faces. However, when they start to howl or show aggression, their true forms are revealed—rotting spirits with burning holes for eyes and ectoplasm drifting from their translucent bodies.

entries.name: Cursed curiosity
entries: Hidden deep within the twisting roots and rotting detritus of the Below, there is a dark cave that sits half in the waking world and half in the Nightmare World. This cave is used as a temple for the rites of undeath by the dread elves of Nod, and there is a heavy curse laid upon interlopers. Sometimes monkeys, curious and unknowing, cross the threshold into the inner sanctum and lose their lives to the dark secrets of undeath, turning mad with the transformation.

entries.name: Confused and enraged
entries: Ghost monkeys are bewildered by the fate that has befallen them, and race from the Nightmare Temple in fear and anger. Unable to eat or sleep, and cast out by their tribes, they roam the Venom Abyss as wretched spirits, hurling phantasmal objects at living creatures and trying to understand a fate their minds could never comprehend.

entries.name: Immortal howls
entries: The anguished howls of the ghost monkey are said to turn even the bravest heart to water. These howls echo across the Venom Abyss at night, and it's not uncommon for a ghost monkey, seeking some kind of cure or answer, to scale the cliffs of the Abyss and escape into the Great Valley, where it hunts and howls in eternal confusion and anger.

entries.name: Giant Armadillo (Glyptodon)
entries: Remarkable for its toughness and cantankerous demeanor, the giant armadillo trudges through a world it wants nothing to do with, sullen and solitary.

Sometimes called a walking boulder, the giant armadillo is a massive, four-legged beast that bears a passing resemblance to a hairy tortoise, with a huge, pebbled shell, armored head and limbs, and a long, spiked tail.

entries.name: Friendless fortress
entries: Few beasts in Planegea are as antisocial as the giant armadillo. Its nearly impenetrable armor and massive size give it the freedom to carve a path through the swamps and riverlands where it finds food, crushing nests and scattering smaller beasts carelessly.

entries.name: Tent stomper
entries: Giant armadillos are known for their particular dislike of mortal encampments. Whether driven by ancestral memory of hunters, a dislike for fire, or sheer ill-will, giant armadillos are known to thunder into peaceful encampments, crushing tents, scattering clanfires, and trampling mortals until driven off or killed.

entries.name: Hunter's prize
entries: Because of their hostility and their shells, which are large enough to use as an armored tent, mortal hunters love to stalk the giant armadillo, considering it a point of pride to bring down such a beast. Clever tactics must be used to expose the creature's weak underbelly, and such a hunt is dangerous—but the dual reward of prized shell and the eradication of a threat to the clanfire is more than enough to trigger a bold hunt.

entries.name: Apex prey
entries: The giant armadillo's enormous size and armor enable it to shake off all but the largest threats. The only creatures the giant armadillo truly fears are terror birds, which stalk it relentlessly, favoring the taste of its meat. Mortal hunting parties would do well to be wary not only of the dangers of the giant armadillo itself, but also the very real threat of a nearby terror bird, hungry for the taste of its salty flesh.

entries.name: Glunch
entries: Deep in the Ghostmire, where undead lights glimmer and giant manylegs slither through the muck, weary wanderers far from the clanfire have stumbled across strange clusters of swamp dwellings inhabited by a green-skinned, black-eyed people known as Glunch.

Glunch are quiet and guarded, slow to interact with outsiders. They prefer to remain hidden, and their naturally slimy green bodies allow them to blend easily into the swamps they inhabit. Their short, muscular forms are topped with large bony spines, used in shows of dominance and a peculiar style of whirling attack in single combat.

entries.name: Ghost-Gardeners
entries: The Glunch have long lived in harmony with the undead spirits that haunt the Ghostmire. They know more ways to pacify angry spirits than any other living thing, and expertly weave subtle enchantments into nets with which they catch the will-o'-the-wisps that illuminate their village clusters. Their mud-and-chitin dwellings are filled with dried roots and herbs, wards, colored cords, and other charms against vengeful ghosts.

entries.name: Manylegs Riders
entries: The Glunch language is a gurgling, clicking sound unintelligible to most. Yet strangely, it enables them to form a rudimentary communication with the giant insects, arthropods, worms, and other creatures collectively known as manylegs. Glunch patrols frequently travel the swamps on the backs of giant millipedes or the huge crabs known as ku-zug. A Glunch rider's bond with its manylegs steed is a strange one—not affectionate, but powerfully codependent.

entries.name: Fungal Memory
entries: The Glunch are known to fear mushrooms and fungus of all kinds. Long ago, their kind had something to do with the mysterious Temple of the Mushroom Lord, and to this day they stay far from that foreboding structure. Glunch ghosts from long ago remember the days when their people served in the temple, and the terrible rites that were enacted there in service of their psychic masters.

entries.name: Horse-Ape (Chalicotherium)
entries: Horse-apes are the unnerving inhabitants of treant groves, traveling alongside the moving forests of Planegea as their companions and protectors. The horse-apes have a rudimentary society, speaking to each other in grunts and whinnies, and glaring with wary, wide-set eyes at any who would approach their beloved trees.

The horse-ape has one of the strangest forms in all Planegea. It towers roughly nine feet tall, with a body much like a ground-sloth or gorilla, topped with the head and neck of a horse. Their coats are striped or spotted with many patterns, and some wear rudimentary crowns or cloaks of leaves and vines given to them by the trees.

entries.name: Tree guards
entries: The horse-apes are by nature gentle grazers, who enjoy ripe fruit and tender grass. Posing no threat to the trees, they were long ago befriended by the forests and their rudimentary intelligence enhanced by the oldest and wisest of the treants, who saw use in their tall and powerful builds. Horse-apes now dwell in and among awakened groves, ready to hurl boulders and bray fiercely at any who come too close to the trees.

entries.name: Reverent followers
entries: Horse-apes are subservient to the trees, worshiping them as gods, and following their orders without questioning. They live simple lives in small family groups, grooming one another and enjoying the bounty of their protected groves, and—when not actively defending the grove—can always be found eating. They are also known to be fond of druids, who share a kinship with some of the trees, and druid groves are not uncommonly guarded by a horse-ape or two bound in service to the trees of that place.

entries.name: Fire-crushers
entries: Through long generations of service to the trees, horse-apes have developed an instinctive fear and hatred of fire, and any whiff of smoke or glimpse of firelight sets them into a warlike rage. Those who would manipulate horse-apes have sometimes surrounded them with rings of torches, which the simple creatures treat as impregnable cages. It's said that there are warlords who have used fire to drive captured horse-apes mad, using these usually docile creatures as berserkers to smash through enemy defenses.

entries.name: Iramuk (Indricotherium)
entries: “Lonely as an iramuk.” That's how the saying goes. These immense beasts tower huge and solitary, dwarfing even the mighty mammoth and tyrantmaw. They move through the world stately and slow, covering huge distances in their lifetime, rarely ever aroused to more than a gentle plod.

In shape, the iramuk is like a rhinoceros with a neck like a giraffe and a long, prehensile upper lip, which they use to dig for roots and strip leaves. Their hide is gray and tough, and their footsteps shake the ground when they pass by.

entries.name: Lonely wanderers
entries: The iramuk is among the most solitary of creatures, only coming together briefly with others of its kind to mate for a few days each year. The rest of its life is spent traversing enormous distances, searching for enough food to sustain its bulk. If two iramuks come across any sign of each other, both will turn perpendicularly outward away from the other, putting as much distance as possible between itself and others of its kind.

entries.name: Living murals
entries: Because of an iramuk's wide range and docile disposition, many of them are painted by various human tribes who encounter them. It is a boast for many young spellskins to draft a spell on the side of an iramuk—a feat that usually requires dangling from a nearby tree or hovering in midair with a long brush. But the act of painting an iramuk is not reserved for spellskins. Druids, shamans, and even ambitious hunters seek to make their mark on the beasts, and most iramuks are patterned with years of paint and dye splashed around their lower bodies, where mortals can reach.

entries.name: Cautious mothers
entries: Iramuks calve only rarely—their mating season is so short that conception is infrequent, and an iramuk spends three years in the womb. Because of the great difficulty of raising offspring and their otherwise solitary lives, iramuk mothers are terrifying when defending their calves, using their incredible size and their long necks as weapons to trample and bash any predator who would threaten their young.

entries.name: Trail makers
entries: Unless they encounter another of their kind, iramuks tend to travel the same path year after year. Their enormous weight and slow steps carve deep furrows in the earth after only a few seasons, and since iramuks plow through brush and hills and open places without slowing their step, their pathways make excellent trails for mortals. Iramuk trails are well known to the local clans that dot their length, and traders favor them to quickly move over long distances in a straight line.

entries.name: Kelodhrosians
entries: The Kelodhrosians are newcomers to the world, emerging from unknown origins in the Venom Abyss. Although they have only existed for a few short generations, they have already created a sprawling network of jungle ziggurats, hastily constructed for their rituals. Their kind is naturally gifted in harnessing the power of primordial evolution, and have learned—whether by instinct or some secret of their creation—how to ritually extract physical aspects from bound victims, stealing bodies to empower their own.

In their natural form, Kelodhrosians are small, hairless, and slender, with long, awkward limbs and white, mask-like faces. Their bulging blue eyes and lipless mouths complete their unsettling visage. But these weak bodies are only the beginning for the Kelodhros—a blank wall on which their story of ascendance will be painted.

entries.name: Unembraced
entries: Kelodhrosians who have yet to Embrace a victim are feeble things, with no natural defenses except their cunning. Unembraced seek to live out of sight in well-camouflaged huts, their white bodies a giveaway for predators in the dark green jungle. From a young age, they are taught to protect themselves with venomous darts—and how to ritually bind a captured victim for the Embrace.

entries.name: Aspecter
entries: Those Kelodhrosians who have Embraced only one or two victims are called aspecters, so called because they display a single aspect of their chosen form. An aspecter looks like an unembraced with some mutation. Some may be stronger, or faster, or have wings, gills, scales, or whatever other aspect they have chosen to extract from their victims.

entries.name: Priest
entries: There are two types of priests, or Kelodhrosians who have Embraced many victims: hybrid priests—who select their preferred aspects from many forms—and monoform priests, who choose to only Embrace a single kind of favored creature, the better to emulate its perfection.
Both are menacing foes, for while hybrid priests have many strengths at their disposal, monoform priests represent discipline, focus, and absolute dedication to their chosen path of ascension.

entries.name: Archpriest
entries: A Kelodhrosian archpriest is one who has completed their ascension, and of whom nothing remains of their original body except their blue eyes. Archpriests rule all lesser Kelodhrosians, and their command of magic and ascension is unparalleled among their own kind.

entries.name: Lost Soul
entries: Some Kelodhrosians lose themselves in the Embrace, due to an interrupted ceremony, an unfit victim, or some other corruption of mind, body, or spirit. Such beings become misshapen monsters, their bodies amorphous and mismatched, their minds broken. The Kelodhros watch over their lost souls, holding their community accountable for the failed Embrace. Such beings often serve archpriests, who are the only ones strong enough to soothe their wild hearts.

entries.name: Infiltrator
entries: A Kelodhros infiltrator is a specialized priest who only Embraces humanoids, the better to blend into a society. Although Kelodhrosians cannot rid themselves of their telltale blue eyes, infiltrators excel at slipping in amongst the clans, finding suitable victims for the Embrace, and scouting out the best locations for advancing colonies of Kelodhros to construct their ritual ziggurats.

race.name: Dreas
alignment: Dreas can be any alignment, but their preference for the unruly side of nature inclines them towards chaotic choices.

entries.name: Age
entries: Dreas live about a century, but they only show their true age on the spring and fall equinoxes. In the fall and winter, they appear to age rapidly. By midwinter, they look 10 years older than their actual age. They then begin to grow younger through the warmer months until midsummer, when they look 10 years younger than their actual age. Then the cycle repeats. In regions with milder or more extreme seasons, this effect is similarly diminished or stronger.

entries.name: Size
entries: Dreas vary greatly in height and build depending on the tree they sprung from, with slender aspen-dreas and massive oak-dreas and everything in between. Your size is Medium.

entries.name: Darkvision
entries: At ease in the gloom of the forest at night, you have superior vision in dark and dim conditions. You can see in dim light within 60 feet of you as if it were bright light, and in darkness as if it were dim light. You can't discern color in darkness, only shades of gray.

entries.name: Botanical Form
entries: Your creature type is plant.

entries.name: Photosynthesis
entries: You require different nourishment than other kinships. Instead of 1 pound of food each day, you require exposure to 1 hour of sunlight, a sunglobe, or an equivalent light source. You cannot eat solid food, and attempting to do so gives you the poisoned condition for 1d4 hours. You require the same amount of water as most humanoid kinships.

entries.name: From the Forest
entries: You know the {@spell druidcraft} cantrip. When you reach 3rd level, you can cast the {@spell speak with plants} spell and the {@spell charm person} spell once per day; you must finish a long rest in order to cast these spells again using this trait. When you cast {@spell charm person} with this feature, sentient plants are also a valid target for the spell. Charisma is your spellcasting ability for these spells.

entries.name: Tree Step
entries: You gain the ability to enter a tree and move from inside it to inside another tree of the same kind within 500 feet. Both trees must be living and at least as large as you. You must use 5 feet of movement to enter a tree. You instantly know the location of all other trees of the same kind within 500 feet and, as part of the movement used to enter the tree, can either pass into one of those trees or step out of the tree you're in. You appear in a spot of your choice within 5 feet of the destination tree, using another 5 feet of movement. If you have no movement left, you appear within 5 feet of the tree you entered. You can use this trait once and regain the ability to do so when you finish a long rest. You must end your turn outside a tree.

entries.name: Languages
entries: You speak Common and Sylvan.

race.name: Half-Ooze
alignment: Half-oozes are, more than anything, hungry. This constant need to consume renders them either neutral, or somewhat evil—although if they can get the food they need through good and lawful means, they may do so.

entries.name: Age
entries: Half-oozes don't last long, since their bodies begin on borrowed time. They usually live out about half their dam's original lifespan, if the dam was young when the half-ooze was sired. A half-ooze with an elvish dam might live for hundreds of years, where one with an orcish dam might die of old age after only a couple of decades.

entries.name: Size
entries: Half-oozes typically range in height from under 4 feet to over 6 feet tall, depending on their dam. Your size is Small or Medium, depending on your dam.

entries.name: Hunger of the Ooze
entries: You must eat every day, or suffer 1 level of exhaustion. When eating, you require twice as much food as other Medium humanoids. You do not require your food to be cooked in order to gain nourishment from it.

entries.name: Pseudopodic Limbs
entries: As an action, you may extend either your arms or legs to twice their normal length. When doing so, your AC decreases by 1. If you extend your legs, your walking speed increases by 10 feet. If you extend your arms, any melee weapon you're wielding is considered to have the Reach property. If a weapon already has the Reach property, it does not gain additional range, as it's too awkward to handle at that length. You may end the effect as a bonus action. Your arms and legs cannot both be extended at the same time.

entries.name: Rubbery
entries: Your ooze origin makes you able to fit through small spaces. Your body can fit through openings normally suitable only for Small or Tiny creatures. Depending on the size of the opening, your armor and equipment may not fit through.

entries.name: Echoes of the Dam
entries: You have the faintest memories from the life of your dam. Choose one: Gain one skill proficiency, one weapon proficiency, or two tool proficiencies.

entries.name: Subtype
entries: Half-oozes are sired by one of three types of ooze: Acidic, Magical, or Sticky. Choose one.

entries.name: Languages
entries: You can speak Common, and can understand one other language of your choice, but you can't speak it.

race.name: Saurian
alignment: Saurians have long memories and hold tightly to many traditions. Their cultures tend towards lawful behavior, but each saurian is an individual with a unique alignment.

entries.name: Age
entries: Saurians reach adulthood at the age of 7 and have exceptionally long lives, surviving as long as gnomes if not killed by predators or disease.

entries.name: Size
entries: Most saurians tend to stand somewhat taller than humans, usually between 6 and 7 feet tall. Your size is Medium.

entries.name: Ancestral Memory
entries: You are connected to the dead, recognizing their guidance and warnings, and know the {@spell sense whispers} cantrip. When you reach 3rd level, you can cast the {@spell bless} spell once with this trait and regain the ability to do so when you finish a long rest. When you reach 5th level, you can cast the {@spell augury} spell once with this trait and regain the ability to do so when you finish a long rest.

entries.name: Subtype
entries: Saurians belong to one of four lines of descent: Leatherwing, Hammertail, Sharpfang, or Webfoot. Choose one.

entries.name: Languages
entries: You can speak Common and Saurian.

race.name: Starling
alignment: Starlings can be any alignment, and react to falling from the sky across the spectrum of selfishness and altruism, order and unpredictability.

entries.name: Age
entries: Starlings have no age, appearing both glamorously young and fabulously mature at the same time. They don't grow older, but they do grow dimmer over the course of their exile from the heavens, eventually fading out over six or seven human lifetimes—although some fade much more quickly due to their sorrow at being far from home.

entries.name: Size
entries: Starlings are generally slender and slightly smaller than other humanoids, usually standing about five feet tall. Your size is Medium.

entries.name: Glow
entries: Choose a color. Your body and your hair (if any) sheds dim light of that color, in a 5 to 20-foot radius. You can alter the radius as a bonus action. This light is permanent as long as you are alive, but a layer of clothing is sufficient to block it.

entries.name: Light-footed
entries: Whenever you make a Charisma ({@skill Performance}) check to dance, you are considered proficient in the Performance skill and add double your proficiency bonus to the check, instead of your normal proficiency bonus.

entries.name: Starry Vigil
entries: On a night where you can see the stars, you do not need to sleep to gain the benefits of a long rest. Instead, you can watch the stars all night, gaining the benefit of a long rest after 8 hours. You remain alert and aware of your surroundings during this time. After finishing this long rest, you have advantage on your next Intelligence (Arcana or Nature) check related to the stars, constellations, or weather.

entries.name: Pure Energy
entries: Due to your stellar nature, you are able to briefly phase out of material existence and pass through obstacles. As a bonus action, using up to 10 feet of your movement, you can become immaterial and pass through all creatures or objects in your path. You may not end these 10 feet of movement inside another creature's space or inside of a solid object. If you attempt to do so, you are pushed back the way you came until you reach an area large enough for your body, and take {@damage 1d10} force damage for each 5 feet you were pushed. You cannot use this trait again until after a short or long rest.

entries.name: Dazzling Touch
entries: Once as an action, you can attempt to blind a creature you can touch with a focused burst of brilliant light. The target must make a Constitution saving throw. The DC is 8 + your Charisma modifier + your proficiency bonus. On a failed save, the target is blinded until the end of your next turn. You cannot use this trait again until after a short or long rest.

entries.name: Languages
entries: You can speak Common and Primordial.

subrace.name: Leatherwing
entries: Leatherwings are reclusive mystics with a reputation for cruelty, pursuing arcane knowledge with a zeal that precludes all other concerns. They are descended from pterosaurs, and have crested heads and long, folding wings attached to their arms.

entries.name: Winged Creature
entries: You gain a flying speed of 45 feet. Flying requires the use of both arms. While flying you can use your action to make a grapple check against another creature of Medium or smaller size with your talons. If you succeed, you may lift and carry the creature with no penalty to your speed.

entries.name: Powerful Aerial Build
entries: You count as one size larger when determining your carrying capacity and the weight you can push, drag, or lift while flying. Half or more of this weight must be hanging from or carried by your talons to take advantage of your aerial build.

subrace.name: Hammertail
entries: Hammertails are contemplative wanderers who never stop moving, preferring to erect temporary shelters only against the most violent storms, and covering enormous ranges in their lifetime journeys. They are descended from ankylosaurs, and have thick, club-like tails.

entries.name: Armored Scales
entries: Your AC increases by 1.

entries.name: Hammer Tail
entries: Your tail is a natural weapon with a reach of 10 feet, which you can use to make unarmed strikes. If you hit with it, you deal bludgeoning damage equal to {@dice 1d4} + your Strength modifier, instead of the bludgeoning damage normal for an unarmed strike.

subrace.name: Sharpfang
entries: Sharpfangs are territorial warriors who live in warlike hunting bands, dedicated to the glory and danger of the chase. They are descended from carnosaurs, and have jagged teeth and long claws.

entries.name: Predatory Rush
entries: When you take the dash action on your turn, your movement speed increases by 10 feet, and your jump distance is doubled for the turn.

entries.name: Hatched to Hunt
entries: Your claws are natural weapons, which you can use to make unarmed strikes. If you hit with them, you deal slashing damage equal to {@dice 1d6} + your Strength modifier, instead of the bludgeoning damage normal for an unarmed strike.

subrace.name: Webfoot
entries: Webfeet are graceful athletes who live on the water where fish and water plants are plentiful, preferring fishing and competitions of strength to the more strenuous life of inland hunters. They are descended from aquatic dinosaurs, and have webbed hands and feet and a high crest on their head.

entries.name: Darkvision
entries: Accustomed to life underwater, you have superior vision in dark and dim conditions. You can see in dim light within 60 feet of you as if it were bright light, and in darkness as if it were dim light. You can't discern color in darkness, only shades of gray.

entries.name: Limited Amphibiousness
entries: You can breathe air and water, but you need to breathe air at least once every 4 hours to avoid suffocating. You gain a swimming speed of 30 feet.

entries.name: Trumpet
entries: As an action, you can make a trumpeting blast of sound that reverberates through your crest. This trumpet can be heard up to a mile away, and up to half as far away if the sound is blocked by solid walls or muffled by other noise such as thunder, battle, hoofbeats, etc. Your trumpet counts as an instrument with which you are proficient.

subrace.name: Acidic
entries: The ooze that sired you was of a peculiarly acidic variety. The skin of acidic half-oozes tends to have a pale greenish hue and a faintly sharp smell hangs around them.

entries.name: Corrosive Rebuke
entries: When you take damage from a melee attack made within 5 feet of you, you can use your reaction to deal acid damage to your attacker. The acid damage is equal to your character level. Once you use this trait, you can't use it again until you finish a short or long rest.

entries.name: Omnivore
entries: You have resistance to acid damage and are immune to being poisoned by food or drink.

subrace.name: Magical
entries: The ooze that sired you was of arcane origin, perhaps concocted by some strange spellskin for unknown experiments. The skin of magical half-oozes tends to have a purplish hue, and they have a vaguely sweet smell.

entries.name: Cantrip
entries: You know one cantrip of your choice from the spellskin spell list. Intelligence is your spellcasting ability for it.

entries.name: Magic Memory
entries: You gain proficiency in the Arcana skill.

subrace.name: Sticky
entries: The ooze that sired you was of a creeping, sticking nature. The skin of sticky half-oozes tends to have a yellowish hue, and they smell faintly of rot.

entries.name: Ooze Climb
entries: Your gummy fingers help you cling to the walls. Climbing no longer costs you extra movement.

entries.name: Sticky Fingers
entries: You gain proficiency in the Sleight of Hand skill.

subclassFeature.name: Dream Sorcery
entries: Just beyond the veil of reality, the magic of dreams shimmer and shift with arcane power. You are touched by this magic and call on the sleeping mind to bring the power of visions and dreams into the waking world. Perhaps dream magic courses through your blood as a birthright, or maybe you gained these powers through a quirk of fate or fortune.

subclassFeature.name: Dream Sorcery Spells
entries: You learn additional spells when you reach certain levels in this subclass, as shown on the Dream Spells table. Each of these spells counts as a sorcerer spell for you, but it doesn't count against the number of sorcerer spells you know. Whenever you gain a sorcerer level, you can replace one spell you gained from this feature with another spell of the same level. The new spell must be an enchantment or an illusion spell from the sorcerer, warlock, or wizard spell list.

entries.caption: Dream Spells
rows: 1st
{@spell heroism}, {@spell dissonant whispers}
3rd
{@spell enhance ability}, {@spell calm emotions}
5th
{@spell hypnotic pattern}, {@spell major image}
7th
{@spell confusion}, {@spell polymorph}
9th
{@spell dream}, {@spell modify memory}

subclassFeature.name: Fate of Dreams
entries: At 1st level, you see portents of things to come in dreams, safeguarding and empowering you and your allies. Upon completing a long rest, choose a number of friendly creatures you can see equal to your Charisma modifier, and roll on the dreams table to determine your dream regarding their fate.

entries.caption: Dreams
rows: Dream of Success. After making a roll but before the outcome is determined, the target adds your proficiency bonus to one ability check.
Dream of Accuracy. After making a roll but before the outcome is determined, the target adds your proficiency bonus to one attack roll.
Dream of Fortitude. After making a roll but before the outcome is determined, the target adds your proficiency bonus to one saving throw.
Dream of Safety. As a reaction when an attack would hit the target, the target adds your proficiency bonus to their AC until the end of their next turn.
Dream of Power. The target adds 2 × your proficiency bonus to one damage roll.
Dream of Speed. The target adds your proficiency bonus to one initiative check.
Dream of Endurance. As a reaction when the target would take damage, the target reduces the damage by an amount equal to 2 × your proficiency bonus.
Dream of Vitality. When the target regains health by spending a hit die, they can regain additional hit points equal to 2 × your proficiency bonus for each use of this feature.

entries: The chosen creatures gain the benefits from this feature until you complete a long rest. Each of those creatures can use this feature once during that time. At 6th level, chosen creatures can use this feature twice, and at 11th level, they can use this feature three times.

subclassFeature.name: Focused Fate
entries: Starting at 6th level, you learn to bend the shape of dreams to affect your fate and the fate of your allies. When you roll for your Fate of Dreams feature, you can roll twice and choose which of the two dreams applies. If you roll the same number on both dice, you can ignore the number and choose any dream on the table.

In addition, once a creature has expended their uses of Fate of Dreams, you can expend a sorcery point to roll on the dream table again, granting them one additional use of the feature with the dream you roll.

subclassFeature.name: Dreamspeaker
entries: Whenever you take a long rest, you may communicate with another creature on the same plane as if by the {@spell dream} spell. You cannot appear as monstrous as part of this effect.

subclassFeature.name: Dreamstep
entries: Starting at 14th level, you are able to swiftly step in and out of the dreaming world. Once on each of your turns, you can use up to 30 feet of your movement to teleport the same distance. You must teleport to an unoccupied space you can see.

subclassFeature.name: Manifest Vision
entries: Starting at 18th level, your wants and dreams are as reality. You can use your action to cast the {@spell simulacrum} spell, targeting yourself. Rather than ice or snow, the simulacrum is composed of the essence of your dreams. The simulacrum you create this way lasts for one hour, after which it dissipates. A simulacrum you create this way can take on any appearance you desire, but cannot be more than one size larger than you. The simulacrum does not have any spell slots above 5th level, and can use metamagic freely without expending any sorcery points, due to the whimsical nature of dreams. In addition, it lacks access to this feature.
Once you use this action, you can't use it again until you finish a long rest, unless you spend 10 sorcery points to use it again.

subclassFeature.name: The Dark Forest
entries: From the heart of the forest, something dark has called to you. An ancient spirit, which harbors no love for things that breathe, sometimes reaches out with magical roots and tendrils to find mortals who will bargain for power to do its bidding. As one chosen by the dark forest, you are sent where the forest cannot reach, into the places where the spirit cannot go, to do its bidding among those it hates.

Consider how you came to have this pact with the dark forest, and why it chose you. Perhaps you entered the pact willingly, with full knowledge of the forest's malevolence, or maybe you simply needed magic to survive or to help others. Regardless, you stand at the edge of something incredibly old, vast, and hostile to your kind, which lets you live and grants you certain powers… for now.

subclassFeature.name: Expanded Spell List
entries: The Dark Forest lets you choose from an expanded list of spells when you learn a warlock spell. The following spells are added to the warlock spell list for you.

entries.caption: Expanded Spells
rows: 1st
{@spell entangle}, {@spell fog cloud}
2nd
{@spell barkskin}, {@spell spike growth}
3rd
{@spell plant growth}, {@spell speak with plants}
4th
{@spell grasping vine}, {@spell hallucinatory terrain}
5th
{@spell awaken}, {@spell tree stride}

subclassFeature.name: Moss Mark
entries: At first level, when you hit a target with an attack, you can mark the injury with magical moss. This moss mark remains for up to one minute, after which it crumbles harmlessly. As a bonus action while you are within 60 feet of a marked creature, you can cause the moss mark to bloom, forcing the target and any other creatures within 10 feet of it to make a Dexterity saving throw versus your spell save DC. On a failed save, a creature takes {@damage 1d6} necrotic damage.
You can leave marks a number of times equal to your proficiency bonus. You regain all uses of this feature upon completing a short or long rest.

subclassFeature.name: Reclaim
entries: At 1st level, you can reclaim those you strike down for the forest. When you reduce a medium or larger creature to 0 hit points, or when a creature marked by your moss dies, you can magically claim it. The claimed body is covered in magical moss, vines, tendrils, and shoots. At the end of 1 minute, the body transforms into a tree one size larger than the creature was in life.

When you create a tree in this way, you can choose to bind it. A tree is bound to you as long as the tree remains standing, or until you dismiss the binding. You can have a number of trees bound to you in this way equal to your proficiency bonus. A bound tree is obedient to you and has limited animation, bending branches and leaves at your request, although it is unable to uproot and move about and cannot attack.

You are able to speak telepathically with a bound tree if you are on the same plane of existence. Bound trees have limited sentience and awareness of their surroundings, and are friendly towards you. Additionally, as an action, you can sense through a bound tree as if standing in the plant's location, choosing one of the following effects until the start of your next turn:

items: You can see, hear, and smell in all directions.
You can sense poison and disease as if casting the {@spell detect poison and disease} spell.
You can sense magic as if casting the {@spell detect magic} spell.

entries: During this time, you are deaf and blind with regard to your own senses, although you remain aware of any pain or damage you suffer.

subclassFeature.name: Oak's Fortitude
entries: Starting at 6th level, you gain toughness from the magic of mighty trees. Your AC can't be less than 16 in any form, regardless of what kind of armor you're wearing. In addition, you have advantage on ability checks and saving throws against being knocked prone, grappled, restrained, or moved against your will.

subclassFeature.name: Dark Bramble
entries: Starting at 10th level, the blooming of your moss mark becomes even more dangerous. When a creature fails their saving throw against your moss mark, in addition to the normal damage, magical brambles erupt from the mark to ensnare them. The creature's speed is reduced to 0 until the end of their next turn.

subclassFeature.name: Wealdwoe
entries: Starting at 14th level, you can call upon the wrath of a spectral forest to destroy your enemies. On your turn, you can use an action to choose a number of points on the ground within 120 feet of you equal to your proficiency bonus. Spectral trees burst from the ground in each of these points, standing up to 60 feet high, with trunks 5 feet in radius, and branches in an additional 15 foot radius. These trees remain for up to one hour, or until you lose concentration, as if concentrating on a spell. If a tree's trunk appears in any creature's space, that creature must make a Dexterity saving throw versus your spell save DC. Creatures marked by your moss have disadvantage on this save. On a failure, the creature takes {@damage 4d10} bludgeoning damage and is thrown up to 30 feet away from the trunk. On a success, the creature takes half as much damage. Structures and objects in the space where the trees appear take full damage.

The trees' branches obstruct vision and movement, providing three-quarters cover, and acting as difficult terrain. Movement through the branches costs 4 feet of movement for every 1 foot of travel. You and any creatures you designate when you create these trees are unaffected by this difficult terrain, as the branches disperse as you pass.
If you concentrate on this effect for the full duration, the spectral trees become permanent, although they no longer provide additional cover or impede movement more than mundane trees.
Once you've used this feature, you can't use it again until you've finished a long rest.

subclassFeature.name: Way of Abnegation
entries: In a raw and dangerous world, some survive through the discipline of self-denial. The way of abnegation is the ascetic practice of denying physical needs and desires to embrace greater clarity of thought, or to unlock a strength buried deep within the core of one's being. An ascetic who has devoted themself to the way of abnegation understands the role of the mind and body as vessels, straining their limits so that they may grow.

This path of denial is different for each that walks it, as sacrifice and hardship take many forms. Some ascetics may practice the disciplines of silence, solitude, and stillness as they seek attunement to the world, and others may train for many days with little food or water, forcing their bodies to endure in accordance with their will. Some distance themselves from their struggle to better understand the world around them, while others embrace their struggle, using their suffering as a catalyst for strength. The way of abnegation is arduous and severe in equal measure, but those who walk it cultivate a great power, drawn up from deep within themselves and honed by the strength of their will to endure.

subclassFeature.name: Discipline of Denial
entries: At 3rd level, your mind and body have grown stronger, and you have learned to endure or to grow stronger in response to the pains of the world. You gain the following benefits:

entries.name: Ward Blows
entries: You can use your reaction to mitigate the strike when you are hit by a melee weapon attack. When you do so, the damage you take from the attack is reduced by {@dice 1d10} + your Dexterity modifier + your monk level. If you reduce the damage to 0, you create an opening for a counterattack. You can spend 1 ki point to make a melee attack against your attacker as part of the same reaction.

entries.name: Abstention
entries: The ki flowing through your body sustains you further than most. You only require half as much food and water to ward off exhaustion. In addition, whenever you complete a long rest, your exhaustion is reduced by 1 additional level.

subclassFeature.name: Enduring Form
entries: Starting at 6th level, you have distanced yourself from the suffering your body endures, or have grown beyond it. Whenever you take the Dodge action, you gain resistance to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage until the start of your next turn.

subclassFeature.name: Prodigious Vessel
entries: Starting at 11th level, the power within and without you has begun to blossom, and you have learned how to best assume the suffering of others.

entries.name: Warding Technique
entries: When a creature you can see attacks a target other than you within 5 feet of you, you can use your reaction to intercept the attack, forcing the attacker to target you instead. You may use your Deflect Missiles or Ward Blows features in response to this attack as part of the same reaction, if possible.

entries.name: Improved Deflection
entries: Whenever you spend a ki point to make either a ranged or melee attack as part of your Deflect Missiles or Deflect Blows features, you make that attack with advantage.

subclassFeature.name: Abdication of Suffering
entries: Starting at 17th level, you have achieved perfect discipline over your physical form. You can take the Dodge action as part of any bonus action you take to make one or more unarmed strikes on your turn.

subclassFeature.name: Path of the Farstriker
entries: Barbarians who follow the Path of the Farstriker seem to be everywhere at once. These instinctive warriors wrap themselves in the primal power of the wind to fill the battlefield with their rage.

Such a barbarian might have cloud giant, storm giant, or djinn blood in their veins. They might come from a part of the world where the boundaries between magic and nature are thin, or they may unknowingly tap into elemental magic to command the air itself in their fury. Consider your barbarian's relationship to the natural world, and how they see the origin of their power.

subclassFeature.name: Farstrike
entries: At 3rd level, you master the technique of flinging weapons at your attackers. You can treat any melee weapons you are holding as if they have the Thrown property with a range of 20/60. If a weapon already had the Thrown property, the short and long ranges of the weapon are doubled. You can draw a weapon that has the Thrown property as part of the attack you make with the weapon.
When you make a ranged weapon attack using your Strength while raging, you can add your Rage Damage bonus to the damage roll.
Starting at 9th level, Brutal Critical applies to ranged weapon attacks you make using your Strength.

subclassFeature.name: Raging Catapult
entries: At 3rd level, you become adept at hurling creatures around the battlefield, whether to harm enemies or reposition allies. Once per turn while you are raging, instead of making a weapon attack, you can hurl a creature within reach up to 30 feet away. Hurled creatures can be no greater than one size larger than you.

An unwilling creature must succeed on a Strength saving throw (DC equal to 8 + your Strength modifier + your proficiency bonus) or be hurled and fall prone, and take bludgeoning damage equal to {@dice 1d6} + your Strength modifier + your Rage damage bonus. Hurling a creature in this way counts as having attacked a hostile creature for the purpose of maintaining your Rage.
After you hurl a friendly creature with this feature, immediately upon landing the creature can use their reaction to make a melee attack.
At 11th level, hurled creatures can be no greater than two sizes larger than you. At 17th level, you can hurl a creature of any size.

subclassFeature.name: Wild Rush
entries: Starting at 6th level, you fill the battlefield with your rushing fury. After you make a thrown weapon attack or use your Raging Catapult feature, you can use a bonus action to take the Dash action. Your jump distance is doubled while dashing.

subclassFeature.name: Boomerang Arc
entries: At 10th level, you have learned to guide your weapon's flight with the wind by your side. When you make an attack with a thrown weapon, you can choose for the weapon to return to your hand after the attack is made.

subclassFeature.name: Tornadic Throw
entries: Starting at 14th level, your thrown weapons summon the terrifying power of the wind at its full force in their wake. While you are raging you can use your action to throw your weapon, creating a line 60 feet long and 5 feet wide out from you in a direction you choose. Each creature in the line must make a Dexterity saving throw (DC equal to 8 + your Strength modifier + your proficiency bonus). On a failed save, a creature takes damage equal to the damage of one of your thrown weapon attacks, and is pushed up to 60 feet towards the end of the line. On a successful save, a creature takes half as much damage and is not pushed. If a creature fails this save by 5 or more, the creature takes additional damage equal to the damage dealt by your Brutal Critical feature. Dealing damage to a hostile creature with this feature counts as having attacked a hostile creature for the purpose of maintaining your Rage.

items.name: Skill Proficiencies:
items.entry: {@skill Acrobatics}, {@skill Investigation}

items.name: Tool Proficiencies:
items.entry: Mender's tools, one type of musical instrument

items.name: Equipment:
items.entry: A net, a set of dark common clothes with pockets, a collapsible pole that extends up to 10 feet, and a belt pouch containing a small antler whistle, a folding toy, and chalk powder worth up to 15 ps in the Great Valley.

entries.name: Feature: Dream Walker
entries: Because of your people's longstanding friendship with the elves of Nod, you have traveled through the World of Dreams more than once. You have stories of your travels through those worlds, and know how to reach out to an elvish contact in Nod, who is friendly and inclined to help you if it does not cause them risk.

entries.name: Suggested Characteristics
entries: Members of the Ape Clan have a reputation for being clever and fearful in equal measure. Children raised in the clan are taught that flexibility and speed are of paramount importance, so Ape Clan members tend to avoid making long-term plans, preferring to keep their options open for whatever the next minute might bring.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: I'm ever-vigilant, constantly looking over my shoulder for signs of danger.
I always have at least two back-up plans.
There's nothing I love more than solving a riddle.
I constantly drum my fingers and fidget with nervous energy.
I have no patience for stubborn, rigid people.
I take the most interesting path, not the most direct.
I always bring up the rear, ready for a quick exit.
I keep quiet until I have the perfect thing to say.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Hope. I must believe that things can get better. (Good)
Cunning. Intellect applied with skill is the only way to keep yourself alive. (Any)
Chance. Nothing is predictable, and any belief otherwise is a delusion. (Chaotic)
Flexibility. It's impossible to plan for the future, all that matters is being ready to react well. (Chaotic)
Order. Anything that can be made clear and straight should be, to push back life's randomness. (Lawful)
Control. In a chaotic world, exerting control is the ultimate power. (Evil)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: I'm loyal to the Ape Clan, and if called upon by my leaders, would go immediately.
I made an oath to my shaman in service of Kho Many-Arms, and I must see it through.
I was chosen by lot to carry out a mission, and since chance has called me, I will go.
There is a great riddle that nobody has been able to solve. I will not rest until I solve it.
I once saw something I can't explain or forget in Nod, and I must return to it.
A loved one was killed by a random accident, and I am bound and determined to find a way to bring them back.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: All planning is stupid. Nothing can be predicted, so it's best to charge in and react to whatever happens.
I always have to prove that I'm the cleverest person in the room.
My instinct is to bolt at the first sign of danger.
I always believe the worst is about to happen.
If things are stable for too long, I have to find a way to shake them up.
I don't trust anyone except myself.

entries: Ah, the Ape Clan. Quick wits, quick bodies... but also quick to scatter when trouble comes. I seek to make peace with all, but I always have trouble counting on Kho's children. Their obsession with fear unnerves me, though I respect their deep connection to the elves of Nod.

items.name: Skill Proficiencies:
items.entry: {@skill Religion}, {@skill Performance}

items.name: Tool Proficiencies:
items.entry: Ceremonial supplies, one type of musical instrument

items.name: Equipment:
items.entry: A set of ceremonial supplies, a set of common clothes, a ceremonial mask honoring the god you served, and a belt pouch of ritual materials worth up to 10 ps in the Great Valley.

entries.name: Feature: Shaman's Aide
entries: You are trained in shaman's work, and understand ceremonies, religious practices, and the duties of shamans intimately. If rituals, festivals, or ceremonies are being prepared, you know how to make yourself useful, and can ingratiate yourself within the hierarchy that serves a god, given enough time to understand the nature of that god.

entries.name: Suggested Characteristics
entries: As an apprentice, you were shaped by the shaman you served under. Those who trained with a gentle, wise shaman are likely to follow in their footsteps, while the former servants of the cruel and tyrannical shy away from anger and harm. Most apprentices are assumed to be meek and mild-mannered, but there is much that brews just under the surface for those who have long been kept quiet.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: I'm always humming a ritual chant under my breath.
I know a ceremony or blessing for every situation.
I don't speak until spoken to.
I don't scare easily in the presence of the supernatural. I've seen too much to be alarmed.
Arcane magic both fascinates and disgusts me.
The only place I feel safe is in a divine hallow. It's mortals that confuse and frighten me.
My training has left me jaded. I revere nothing.
I feel guilty and stressed when I go for long without revering a god.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Faith. Believing in a higher power is what enables people to achieve great things. (Good)
Comfort. One should seek the easy way where possible. (Neutral)
Reverence. That which is powerful is worthy of honor. (Lawful)
Freedom. Life is too short to live under the thumb of another, mortal or divine. (Chaotic)
Tradition. The old ways are inherently worthy of being upheld. (Lawful)
Power. The ability to bend others to your will is all that matters in the world. (Evil)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: My shaman died under strange circumstances. Only I know the truth of what happened.
I broke my god's taboo and was exiled. I long to return to their service.
I escaped from an evil shaman and have sworn never again to bend the knee in reverence to any deity.
My shaman uttered a prophecy over me, and I am bound and determined to fulfill it at all costs.
I befriended a minor god and seek to advance their glory by any means possible.
I am wanted dead by a certain cult for crimes that I have not yet committed.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I defer too quickly to others, even when I disagree.
I trained with the best and know the most, and everyone can benefit from my insight on every topic.
I keep a mental list of everyone who has ever wronged me. Someday, each and every one of them will pay.
I'm far too trusting of everyone.
I complain when I'm tired, hungry, bored, lonely, confused, irritated, thirsty, sweaty, cold…
It's difficult not to judge others when I'm obviously better than they are.

entries: Many apprentices have I trained. Some were talented, some were not. The only ones I remember were those who understood that even the mightiest shaman was once no more than they are, and spoke to me as a person as well as a mentor.

items.name: Skill Proficiencies:
items.entry: {@skill Athletics}, {@skill Animal Handling}

items.name: Tool Proficiencies:
items.entry: Cook's utensils, butcher's tools

items.name: Equipment:
items.entry: A club, a set of common clothes, a bone trophy from a bear-wrestling competition, and a belt pouch containing honeycomb, spices, and patches of pelt worth up to 15 ps in the Great Valley.

entries.name: Feature: Master of the Feast
entries: You know how to bring people together around a table. With sufficient supplies and time, you can prepare an impressive meal. Where food is plentiful, you know how to be an entertaining host, and can earn the favor of strangers with jokes, boasting, and good cheer around the table over the course of a meal.

entries.name: Suggested Characteristics
entries: The Bear Clan is seen as a bunch of boastful, gluttonous brawlers—and the best of allies to have by your side in a fight. Known for their gregarious spirits, love of laughter and feasts, and irrepressible nature, the Bear Clan is the heart of the Great Valley. Not everyone from the Bear Clan is equally bold and boisterous, but an undercurrent of confidence is a hallmark of the children of the Unkillable.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: I love a good boast, and practice my own boasting at every opportunity.
I go with my gut, preferring not to overthink things.
I always assume everyone around me is looking to me for leadership.
Food is the language by which I communicate love.
I always say exactly what's on my mind.
I'm friendly with everyone but close to nobody.
If a riddle or puzzle can't be solved in ten seconds, it's a waste of everyone's time and should be smashed.
I'm always the last to go to bed, preferring to talk by the fire until everyone else turns in.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Joy. Happiness is the light that pushes back the darkness. (Good)
Plenty. Abundance is the greatest sign of blessing and success one could hope for. (Any)
Strength. I respect strength, and bow the knee to the one who is stronger than me. (Lawful)
Laughter. It's worth breaking the rules to spread joy. (Chaotic)
Life. All life should be celebrated and protected. (Good)
Oppression. The strong have been chosen by fate to subject the weak to their will. (Evil)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: I once earned the title of champion wrestler and will defend it at every opportunity.
I seek the secret of true immortality and invulnerability.
I made an ill-considered joke at the expense of a powerful hunter and they want me dead to this day.
A captive of our clan told me a terrible secret, which started me on the path I now pursue.
I have sworn an oath that I will do all in my power to feed the hungry and bring joy to the wretched.
I'm fascinated with giant culture, and secretly admire the empires.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I tend to act like I'm invincible.
Self-control? What's that?
I can be a bully, often without even realizing it.
I can't say no to a challenge or dare.
Any sign of weakness earns my scorn.
I attack first and ask questions later... or never.

entries: Some call the Bear Clan the “little giants.” They live at the feet of the Stone Empire, and there is hardly anything but height separating the raiders of that land from the hunting parties of the Bear people.

items.name: Skill Proficiencies:
items.entry: {@skill Stealth}, {@skill Perception}

items.name: Tool Proficiencies:
items.entry: Mender's tools

items.name: Languages:
items.entry: One of your choice

items.name: Equipment:
items.entry: Mender's tools, a set of tattered clothes, a handheld keepsake of your original home, and ragged cast-offs stolen from your captors worth up to 5 ps in the Great Valley.

entries.name: Feature: Overheard Secret
entries: Through your time in forced service, you overheard valuable secret knowledge that may be of great use to you or to others. This could be unknown lore, the location of an object of great value or power, dangerous plans, a weakness that can be exploited, or some other secret. Work with your DM to determine exactly what you overheard.

entries.name: Suggested Characteristics
entries: Being captured and held against your will can happen to anyone, and can have myriad effects on personality, outlook, and character. Former captives have been through true suffering and terror, and may be meek or aggressive, composed or wild. Some have sworn powerful oaths during their times of captivity that they must see through, others wish nothing more than to simply live as they see fit in the moment.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: I am jumpy and skittish after years of being in danger.
I've seen it all and can't be frightened by anything.
When I fight, I recklessly unleash all the pent-up fury and rage from my time of captivity.
Dignity is what got me through my captivity, and I remain composed no matter what.
I watch others closely, reading the subtlest moods and mirroring them.
I stay out of everyone's way, preferring to hang just out of sight.
I make do with what I have and never ask anyone for anything.
There's nothing I love more than stories of travel and adventure, having lived on them when I was captured.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Freedom. Everyone should be free to do as they see fit at all times. (Chaotic)
Revenge. I'll make those who hurt me suffer double for everything they did to me. (Evil)
Restoration. Those who suffer should be made whole and reunited with those they love. (Good)
Protection. Evil should be toppled so that nobody has to go through what I went through. (Good)
Justice. Evil should be punished and good rewarded in exact measure. (Lawful)
Security. Being safe from those who would do you harm is the most important thing. (Any)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: I was abducted from my family and am seeking them.
I will not stop until I kill my captor.
I got out, but I left someone behind. I have to go back and bring them out to freedom too.
There's something that kept me alive in captivity, something I promised myself I'd do. I have yet to do it.
I still have nightmares about my captivity, and sometimes can't remember that I'm free upon waking.
I'll take any and every opportunity to cause harm to those who remind me of my captors.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I secretly resent those who have had an easier life.
There's some part of me that misses my captive life, and I hate myself for that.
I can be cruel and insensitive, thanks to my years of living so close to suffering.
Now that I'm free, I refuse to accept any boundaries, even when they're for my own good.
I don't know how to ask for help.
My anger flares hot and fierce at the smallest insult.

items.name: Skill Proficiencies:
items.entry: {@skill Medicine}, {@skill Insight}

items.name: Tool Proficiencies:
items.entry: Cook's utensils, gaming set, mender's tools

items.name: Equipment:
items.entry: Cook's utensils, a child's toy, a soft blanket, a set of common clothes, and a caretaker's satchel containing thread, a bone needle, soothing balm, clean leaves, a ration of nuts and dried berries, and materials worth up to 10 ps in the Great Valley.

entries.name: Feature: Good With Children
entries: Young children instinctively like and trust you. They are eager to involve you in their games and tell you secrets, show you things they are proud of, and take actions to please you and win your approval. You are able to calm and soothe an upset child quickly, and have an instinctive sense of whether young children are being well cared for or mistreated.

entries.name: Suggested Characteristics
entries: Caretakers are typically canny, observant, and resourceful people who have spent a lot of time in a secret world with those in their charge, unknown to other adults. This special bond affects the way they look at life, giving them an unusual and valuable perspective.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: I'm incredibly proud of my companions, and never miss a chance to tell them so.
There's nothing you need that isn't in my satchel.
Guilt is a weapon that I'm proficient with.
I can't stand it when those I love are in conflict, and will do anything to keep the peace.
I am capable and confident in every situation.
Everything reminds me of the children I've cared for.
There's no bad situation that can't be made better with snacks.
I'm a good listener, and people like to tell me their problems.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Truth. Honesty is the best policy, even when it hurts. (Lawful)
Joy. There is so much good in the world if we'll just open our eyes to see it. (Good)
Creativity. Self-expression is how we conquer the darkness. (Chaotic)
Order. There is a place for everything, and everything should be in its place. (Lawful)
Peace. Nothing is more important than serenity and balance. (Neutral)
Growth. Experiences good and bad all shape us and make us stronger. (Any)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: No matter how old you are, if I once cared for you, you'll always be my child.
A child I raised grew up to be evil. I live to undo the harm they caused.
My charge was not my own child, but their family has become my family.
Living as a caretaker has made me realize the life debt that I owe to the person who raised me.
I will stop at nothing to destroy that which threatened my child.
I fight for all children everywhere.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I am sensitive to the needs of others, so much so that I often forget to take care of myself.
I pretend like everything is fine even when it isn't.
Past disrespect has made me defensive about my skills and knowledge.
Everything is my business, and I don't mind telling you what I think whether you want to hear it or not.
I feel like I have to prove myself to other adults.
I oversimplify problems, looking for quick solutions even when there are none.

entries: To spend your life in service of the smallest, the least, the weakest. Could there be anything more profound, more magnificent?

entries.name: Variant: Caretaker of the Infirm
entries: Instead of children, you were the guardian of the elderly and infirm, those who could no longer hunt or contribute to the council of elders. You spent days in the cool of the tents or huts of your people, listening to stories and helping alleviate discomfort both physical and mental.

Discuss who was under your care with your DM, and decide who their living relatives are, and what their relationship with your charge was. Were they kind and concerned, or distant and disdainful? Do they trust you or hold you in suspicion? Did your charge let slip any important family secrets?

Rather than proficiency with a gaming set, you might have proficiency with a musical instrument of your choice. Your skill with children becomes similar skill with the elderly. And instead of a children's toy, you can start with a bundle of beaded and painted sticks, which you can use to reenact the many stories handed down to you by your former ward.

items.name: Skill Proficiencies:
items.entry: {@skill History}, {@skill Persuasion}

items.name: Tool Proficiencies:
items.entry: Ceremonial supplies, butcher's tools

items.name: Equipment:
items.entry: A symbol of authority (such as a headdress, staff, or pendant), a set of fine clothes, an intricately carved piece of jade-and-ivory given to you by the chieftain, and a belt pouch containing rare spices, crystals, and finely-worked arrowheads worth up to 25 ps in the Great Valley.

entries.name: Feature: Clan Deference
entries: You carry an air of authority that's obvious for all to see. Low-status clan members seek to earn your favor and defer to you on decisions, and high-status clan members recognize you as worthy of their attention. People assume you have the right to be wherever you are, and you can gain the right to approach the chieftain of a local clan.

entries.name: Suggested Characteristics
entries: Those who have spent much time in the tents of the powerful understand the ways in which power flows. Each chieftain's family is different, but they are often seen as privileged, used to comfort and deference, and skilled in a certain degree of scheming and politics.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: I constantly drop names about the important people I've spent time with.
I give great care and attention to my appearance, dressing as well as possible in every situation.
As someone with experience in important decisions, I dole out lots of advice, whether I'm asked for it or not.
I have a few creature comforts that I cannot do without.
I'm endlessly fascinated by the romantic lives of the common folk.
I'm deaf to insults, interpreting them as veiled compliments.
I have endless stories about the scandalous lives of the chieftains and shamans I have known.
I don't like to actually be the person in charge—just to have influence over them.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Authority. There is a hierarchy, and status organizes life as it should be lived. (Lawful)
Family. Blood is everything. (Any)
Responsibility. Those who possess power have a calling to care for those without it. (Good)
Individuality. A person should be free to live as they choose no matter who they are. (Chaotic)
Respect. Those with titles or power should be shown proper honor and deference. (Lawful)
Selfishness. I do what I want and get what I want, without regard for others. (Evil)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: My family member, the chieftain, is my first and highest loyalty. I will do as they desire.
I have seen abuses of power in the tents of the mighty, and seek to root out tyranny wherever I encounter it.
If the chieftain dies, I will inherit control of the clan. I'm not sure that I want it.
The shaman cursed me as part of a power struggle with the chieftain. I travel to break the curse.
My family decided I'm too sheltered to lead the clan, so I'm traveling to prove that I can survive anything.
I ran away from the clan and want nothing to do with it. They're still seeking to drag me back.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I was raised among the powerful and consider everyone else beneath me.
I automatically believe that everyone wants to please and protect me.
I steal things because I assume everything already belongs to me.
I despise the powerful, and don't trust anyone in a position of authority.
I intentionally manipulate others to get what I want, and don't feel the slightest remorse about it.
I say whatever it takes to get others to like me.

items.name: Skill Proficiencies:
items.entry: {@skill Sleight of Hand}, {@skill Investigation}

items.name: Tool Proficiencies:
items.entry: Choose two of these types of crafter's tools: stonecrafter, bonecrafter, woodcrafter, or leatherworker

items.name: Equipment:
items.entry: A set of crafter's tools, a set of common clothes, a specialized tool you invented that makes your craft easier, and a belt pouch containing leather straps, obsidian flakes, oils, adhesives, and other supplies worth up to 20 ps in the Great Valley.

entries.name: Feature: Craftwork
entries: Your skills are useful no matter where you go, and you can always find enough work among other mortals to earn you a night's rest and sufficient food and drink for the day. This work may involve repairs, advising, skilled labor, or enhancing existing structures or crafted items. In addition, your work may be recognized by other practitioners of your chosen craft, and you are generally respected by common folk.

entries.name: Suggested Characteristics
entries: Focused, precise, and a bit obsessive, crafters are known for their tireless efforts to build, combine, and construct materials that enhance their odds of survivals and raise the standard of living for their clans. Crafters go far beyond shaping knives and tanning hides. They construct elaborate dwellings, clever traps, and specialized tools, and are generally thought to have a certain degree of ego about their skills.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: I'm always working away at some small part of a larger construction.
I gather inspiration wherever I go, studying the details of nature to better devise new work.
I have never given up on anything I've set my mind to.
I keep a sharp eye out for the things my friends might need, and work quietly to make or obtain them.
I can't help trying to improve everything I come across, just a little bit.
I keep a mental list of the strengths and flaws of others, as if they were crafted objects to be evaluated.
I love exploiting a weakness for advantage.
I never shy away from an opportunity to prove how clever I am.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Skill. Craft is to be respected for its own sake, and developing it is the greatest good. (Any)
Beauty. It is right to make things that are lovely and uplifting. (Good)
Reason. Everything can be thought through if rightly considered and properly ordered. (Lawful)
Ego. Everyone can, should, and will bow before my genius. (Evil)
Reputation. Your name is all you really have. Best make it mean something. (Any)
Discovery. Any mess made in the process of learning something new is worth it. (Chaotic)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: I am always pursuing the creation of my masterpiece.
Everything I do is to show up a rival crafter.
I am carrying on a family tradition, and each generation of my family becomes more skilled in our craft.
I dreamed of a strange object that I am trying to recreate, not knowing what it does.
I seek to learn from other crafters as I travel, gleaning what knowledge I can wherever I go.
A weapon I created was used to murder someone I loved deeply. I now make things as an act of penance.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I consider myself the world's greatest living expert on everything.
Sometimes I lose myself in certain obsessions.
I am hesitant to act until I've logically evaluated every possibility.
I secretly hoard valuable materials in case I need them.
I think of others as tools, and only value them according to the use I have for them.
I don't bother explaining my actions to others—it's easier just to do things myself.

items.name: Skill Proficiencies:
items.entry: {@skill Sleight of Hand}, {@skill Deception}

items.name: Tool Proficiencies:
items.entry: One type of gaming set

items.name: Languages:
items.entry: One of your choice

items.name: Equipment:
items.entry: A scouting hoop, a set of common clothes, a woven bag with a concealed pocket at the bottom, and a belt pouch containing wooden jewelry, dried jungle berries, and carved bone figurines worth up to 15 ps in the Great Valley.

entries.name: Feature: Settlement Savvy
entries: You know your way around large settlements and crowds of people. You can make your way through crowded areas easily, and know how to find places in cities where people gather to drink, rest, or rule. You can quickly make an acquaintance in a new city or large settlement who will give you a broad idea of the layout and politics of the place. Additionally, you can gather and entertain a small crowd on the street with sleight of hand and party tricks for up to five minutes.

entries.name: Suggested Characteristics
entries: Anyone from Edgegather is assumed to be a drunkard, a swindler, a show-off, and an entertaining companion with more good stories than just about anyone. In a city given to revelry, most survive by adopting a festive mood—even if it masks darker secrets below the surface.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: I lean into an intoxicated act. It throws my enemies off.
I always have a trick, a joke, and a con at the ready.
I am the life of the party. And if there isn't a party, I'll start one.
I hang back while others celebrate. I've seen too many festivities go wrong to let down my guard.
I treat everything as a joke, including death.
I steal things absentmindedly.
There's nothing I like more than watching others get into trouble.
Life is too short and pointless to be anything less than heroically gutsy.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Trickery. If I can fool you, you deserved it. (Chaotic)
Cunning. Survival belongs to those smart enough to stay one step ahead. (Any)
Pleasure. There is no higher good than the hunt for happiness, ease, and wellbeing. (Neutral)
Courage. The best of us stare down danger with unblinking eyes. (Good)
Greed. There's nothing I want that shouldn't belong to me. (Evil)
Civilization. As one rises, we all rise. (Lawful)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: I owe a debt to a collector back in Edgegather, and can't show my face there again without payment.
I'm something of a local celebrity for catching a child who toppled over the cliff edge.
I'm working to complete a series of drunken pledges I made while reveling in the streets of the city.
My origins in the Drunken City are an embarrassment, and I prefer that nobody knows I'm from there.
I am betrothed to one or two people back in the city, and it's best for everyone if they never find me again.
I went on several ventures into the Venom Abyss, and still feel the call of the jungle to this day.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I lie by force of habit, even when there's nothing to be gained by it.
I assume everyone is always running a con.
There's no problem that a strong drink can't solve.
I'm addicted to games of chance.
Values are for those who can afford them. I'll do anything for a big enough reward.
I enjoy making fools of others… even those I care about.

entries: They say never go to Edgegather unless you're willing to lose your goods, your dignity, or your life. It's the lucky who only lose one.

items.name: Skill Proficiencies:
items.entry: {@skill Arcana}, {@skill Insight}

items.name: Tool Proficiencies:
items.entry: Thieves' tools

items.name: Languages:
items.entry: One of your choice

items.name: Equipment:
items.entry: A staff or lever, a set of common clothes, a giant's ring worn as an armband, and a belt pouch containing woven cloth, salt, and chipped gemstones worth up to 15 ps in the Great Valley.

entries.name: Feature: Inspiring Reputation
entries: Mortals everywhere have heard the tale of Free Citadel and its overthrow of the giant lords who ruled it. The first time a group of mortals hears you come from the Citadel, they are likely to be friendly towards you and provide you with food, drink, and other easily-obtained provisions, if they have some to spare, in exchange for your account of the city and its liberation. Additionally, you can give a rousing speech about defeating impossible odds and stir a crowd to heroics, recruiting a handful of common mortals to fight alongside you—though they may flee quickly once real combat starts.

entries.name: Suggested Characteristics
entries: Those who dwell within the towering walls of the mountain citadel are considered leaders and heroes to the people of the Great Valley—living proof that the giant empires need not oppress mortals forever. The archetypal Free Citizen is bold and upright, committed to freedom and loyalty, with a little giantish wealth and knowledge to throw around and a bit of a tendency to show off.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: I know little of the world outside the citadel's walls and am surprised by common occurrences.
I always have an encouraging word for the oppressed.
I'm something of a show-off.
I am unused to gods and shamans, and often give offense where none is meant.
I consider myself an expert on giant lore.
I mention my origin every chance I get.
I find sleeping outside stone walls unnerving.
I consider everywhere but Free Citadel to be backwards and uncouth.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Freedom. Tyranny should be toppled, and the oppressed liberated. (Good)
Discovery. Secrets are made to be discovered, and doors to be opened. (Any)
Hope. Even in darkness, hope holds on. (Good)
Destruction. Shattering what others build is the final act of power. (Evil)
Loyalty. There is no closer bond than faithfulness to those you have sworn to serve. (Lawful)
Life. As grass grows through the slabs of a ruin, so life everywhere pushes through oppression. (Chaotic)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: I idolize the Usurper Queen, and would do anything to gain her favor.
I am afflicted by a cursed giant artifact and am seeking to break the spell.
There is a vault in the Citadel which holds a great secret. I journey to find the magic word to open it.
I travel to spread the word of overthrow and incite rebellion against the giants wherever I can.
I dabbled in giant magic too great and dark for me and was exiled from the Citadel forever on pain of death.
I believe the Citadel can be the start of a new mortal empire and am recruiting warriors to join it.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: Some say I'm overconfident, but if my people can slay giants, there's nothing I can't do.
I poke my nose where it doesn't belong.
I take charge and tell others what they can and can't do.
I like to start fights just to prove my courage.
I tend to hoard goods and information.
I am used to refined living in the Citadel, and hate filth or dirt of any kind.

entries: If a single flower of hope blooms anywhere in Planegea, it is Free Citadel. Yet the roots of that flower go deep into the mountain, and there are secrets buried there to curdle the blood.

items.name: Skill Proficiencies:
items.entry: {@skill Nature}, {@skill Survival}

items.name: Tool Proficiencies:
items.entry: Gatherer's supplies, mapmaker's tools

items.name: Equipment:
